references.name: Christological theology (Jesus as gentle and humble)
references.context: The sermon's Christology emphasizes Jesus's humility as an essential attribute linked to his dependence on the Father and grounding in human suffering and joy.

references.name: Etymology of 'humility' from Latin 'humus' (ground/earth)
references.context: Preacher notes that 'the root of the word humble is humus, the ground, the earth,' connecting linguistic meaning to spiritual truth about groundedness.

references.name: Broderick Greer (colleague, not intellectual authority)
references.context: Named as someone who worked with the person in recovery, included as a pastoral memorial ('Rest his soul'), not cited as an intellectual source.

primary_theme: Humility, understood biblically and monastically as a spiritual power rather than weakness, is the golden thread connecting all theological readings and the foundation of the Christian life, exemplified in Jesus's gentle invitation and practiced through groundedness in shared human experience.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection combined with scholarly theological argument; the preacher moves between personal anecdote (AA meeting, seminary memory), expert testimony (Dr. Good's teaching), historical-traditional authority (Desert Fathers and Mothers), and scriptural exegesis, building toward spiritual invitation and memorization of Gospel text.

references.name: Annie Dillard
references.context: American author who won a Pulitzer Prize, cited as 'one of my favorite authors' known for deep, intense writing; her autobiography 'An American Childhood' is described as 'wonderful.'

references.name: Holy the Firm
references.context: Short book by Annie Dillard about human suffering; contains an anecdote about a priest spontaneously interrupting prayers of the people to say 'Lord, we bring you these same petitions every week.'

references.name: An American Childhood
references.context: Autobiography by Annie Dillard described as 'riveting and hilarious' and 'wonderful' - contrasted with 'Holy the Firm' as lighter beach reading.

references.name: Psalm 23
references.context: Referenced as a comforting psalm in contrast to Psalm 69, which 'doesn't sound at all like' it.

references.name: Psalm 69
references.context: Primary biblical text analyzed in the sermon; described as sounding like a diary entry of frustration with family, containing the plea 'Hide not your face from your servant. Be swift and answer me, for I am in distress.'

references.name: Psalm 139
references.context: Cited as the preacher's favorite psalm, described as 'lofty' and conveying 'God's presence being with you wherever you may be.'

references.name: Liturgical tradition / Liturgy
references.context: The preacher references 'the great tradition of liturgy' and notes that prayers of the people are always in the vernacular, even when other liturgy is in Greek or Latin.

references.name: Vernacular prayer tradition
references.context: The preacher explains that prayers of the people must always be in the vernacular—'our own language' and 'our own words'—as an ancient and essential liturgical principle.

references.name: Anglican/Episcopal tradition
references.context: The sermon reflects Episcopal/Anglican liturgical practice and theology; references to 'Holy Communion,' 'the Eucharist,' and 'Canon Broderick' editing prayers within this tradition.

references.name: Unnamed Anglican priest theologian and literary scholar
references.context: Cited as authority for the claim that prayers of the people are 'primarily an offering and not a request'; preacher says 'It doesn't matter who he is' but uses him to define the nature of intercessory prayer.

references.name: Canon Broderick
references.context: An editor of the prayers of the people at the preacher's church who 'makes them poetic,' ties them to liturgical year and biblical themes.

primary_theme: The prayers of the people in the liturgy are an essential form of authentic, direct address to God that move from the personal and intimate to the universal, requiring genuine words spoken in one's own language rather than formulas.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with narrative storytelling; the preacher uses personal anecdotes (the Annie Dillard story, the shy Episcopalian), biblical analysis (Psalm 69), and theological explanation to make accessible a liturgical principle about authentic prayer.

references.name: W.H. Auden
references.context: Auden, identified as 'the great poet,' is cited as being fascinated by Jesus's command 'Follow me' and its parallel to John 14:6, and wrote a poem about these two verses that is set to music and appears as hymn 464 in the church hymnal.

references.name: Matthew (Tax Collector)
references.context: The gospel narrative where Jesus says 'Follow me' to Matthew, a tax collector, which the narrator notes is 'not a compliment.'

references.name: John 14:6
references.context: Jesus's statement 'I am the way, the truth, and the life,' paired by Auden with the 'Follow me' passage as parallel verses about discipleship.

references.name: King James Version (Bible translation)
references.context: The preacher notes that in the King James Version, Jesus's command is rendered as 'Follow thou me.'

references.name: Hymn 464
references.context: Auden's poem set to music, found in the church hymnal, which the preacher encourages the congregation not to look up during the sermon (with humorous consequence noted from the 8 o'clock service).

references.name: St. Mark's Episcopal Church (The Bowery, Manhattan)
references.context: Identified as Auden's church home where he was a member of the congregation, establishing his connection to the Episcopal tradition.

references.name: Book of Common Prayer - Psalter (Drafting Committee)
references.context: Auden served on the drafting committee for the Psalter section of the Book of Common Prayer, contributing to the biblical poetry heard in Sunday worship, baptisms, and burials.

references.name: The Psalter
references.context: The biblical poetry section of the Book of Common Prayer that is read and sung every Sunday, every burial and baptism, with Auden having contributed to its drafting.

references.name: The Gospels (general narrative)
references.context: The sermon treats the gospel reading as 'essentially a bundle of several stories' containing various people: tax collectors, sinners, a father whose daughter is dying, and a woman with a hemorrhage for twelve years.

references.name: Matthew's Gospel (Tax Collector narrative)
references.context: The preacher notes that 'the narrator of this gospel, Matthew's gospel' identifies Matthew as a tax collector in a way that is clearly not complimentary.

references.name: Gospel healing narratives (daughter of Jairus and woman with hemorrhage)
references.context: The sermon references a father whose daughter is 'in the grip of death' and 'a woman who's been suffering from hemorrhage for 12 years,' noting that 'we often think things happen in the Gospels quickly, but not that much.'

references.name: Auden's poem - 'Christ is the way / Follow him through the land of unlikeness / You will see rare beasts and have unique adventures'
references.context: The preacher quotes directly from Auden's hymn-poem to illustrate the nature of Christian discipleship as a journey through strange and unique experiences.

references.name: The concept of 'the land of unlikeness'
references.context: Auden's phrase 'land of unlikeness' (echoing Plotinus and later used in medieval theology) describes the space of spiritual struggle and transformation that discipleship requires.

references.name: Episcopal liturgical tradition
references.context: The sermon is grounded in Episcopal worship practices including the Book of Common Prayer, hymnal usage, communion, blessings, and the role of altar participation.

primary_theme: Following Christ is a unique, transformative journey that calls us into relationship with people of all kinds—those we would not naturally choose—mirroring Jesus's radical inclusion of tax collectors and sinners, and requiring both inner spiritual exploration and outward acts of hospitality and listening.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative storytelling combined with literary allusion; the preacher uses Auden's poetry as an intellectual framework, then grounds the abstract theology in two concrete pastoral vignettes (a homeless man seeking to be heard, and a toddler's wordless encounter) to illustrate the sermon's claims about Christian community and inclusion.

references.name: John's Gospel
references.context: The Easter gospel reading comes from John; the preacher took a graduate seminar on John's gospel and references its 'mysteries and intricacies' throughout.

references.name: John 20:11-18 (Mary Magdalene at the tomb)
references.context: The primary text analyzed: Mary Magdalene weeping at the tomb, turning around, and encountering the risen Christ who calls her by name.

references.name: Good Friday liturgy/readings
references.context: Referenced through Canon Katie's Good Friday sermon and the fact that Mary was present at the crucifixion 'a stone's throw away from the cross' with Jesus' mother.

references.name: Psalm 30:5
references.context: Quoted directly: 'Weeping may spend the night, but joy comes in the morning' as evidence of resurrection experience.

references.name: John the Baptist / Repentance imagery
references.context: Referenced negatively to clarify that Mary's turning around is NOT repentance (turning away from sin) but rather a turn toward God's presence while holding her grief.

references.name: Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.
references.context: Quoted: 'the arc of the moral universe is long, but it bends toward justice,' cited as proof that God's work of justice is real and ongoing.

references.name: New Testament scholarship
references.context: The preacher's professor had a PhD in New Testament and taught 'scholarly' commentary on John's gospel; represents academic theological approach.

references.name: Homiletical tradition / preaching theory
references.context: The professor's advice: 'stop trying to be profound. Try to be helpful.' Represents a specific homiletical philosophy about Easter preaching.

references.name: Historical-critical methodology
references.context: The preacher's youthful desire to document the resurrection 'historically and academically' like a historian would, using video evidence to make it 'credible and coherent and rational.'

references.name: Religious studies as academic discipline
references.context: The preacher majored in religious studies at a public university, representing secular academic approach to faith questions.

references.name: Twelve-step recovery tradition
references.context: Referenced implicitly through mention of addiction recovery: 'hit rock bottom and inexplicably turn to a higher power, find a recovery group.'

references.name: Mystical theology / contemplative spirituality
references.context: The emphasis on the 'human heart and mind when fully engaged' experiencing resurrection through interior transformation rather than external evidence.

primary_theme: Resurrection is not a historical event to be proven but a spiritual reality to be experienced through grief, attention, and turning toward God's presence, as exemplified by Mary Magdalene's Easter witness.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection with personal testimony, scholarly anecdote, and prophetic application; combines academic memory (graduate school) with lived pastoral experience to move from intellectual skepticism toward contemplative faith.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: Preacher notes that the nativity narrative with manger scene, angels, and shepherds appears only in Luke and Matthew, not in John.

references.name: Gospel of Matthew
references.context: Cited alongside Luke as the only gospels containing the traditional nativity narrative with barnyard animals and heavenly hosts.

references.name: Gospel of John
references.context: John 1:1-5 quoted and analyzed: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God' — presented as a philosophical rather than narrative account of Jesus's incarnation.

references.name: Gospel of Mark
references.context: Mentioned in passing as one of the four gospels in chronological discussion of gospel composition.

references.name: Biblical Incarnation theology
references.context: The theological concept that 'the Word became flesh' — central to John's prologue and the sermon's meditation on Christ's incarnation as universal rather than particular.

references.name: Born again/rebirth theology
references.context: The preacher invokes the spiritual principle of being 'born again' and 'renewed' as the invitation extended by John's gospel on Christmas morning.

references.name: Johannine theology / High Christology
references.context: The preacher describes John's approach as emphasizing Christ's cosmic significance ('through this Word all things were created') and universality rather than the particularity emphasized in Matthew and Luke.

references.name: Gospel composition criticism / Synoptic problem
references.context: The preacher cites historical-critical scholarship: 'Historians believe...that John was written last, well after Matthew, Mark, and Luke,' suggesting John knew the other nativity accounts and chose an alternative approach.

references.name: Episcopal theology of ongoing spiritual renewal
references.context: The preacher notes the specifically Episcopal interpretation: 'for the Episcopalians...we would add to be born again, not just once, but over and over again throughout the course of a lifetime.'

primary_theme: John's gospel offers a philosophical, universal meditation on the Word becoming flesh and the cosmic significance of Christ's incarnation, contrasting with Matthew and Luke's particular, narrative nativity accounts, and inviting believers to continuous spiritual rebirth.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling combined with scholarly biblical analysis; the preacher uses a personal anecdote about a rural nativity scene to set up a sophisticated comparison of gospel literary traditions, moving from the concrete and colorful to the abstract and philosophical.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: Preacher contrasts Luke's nativity narrative (with manger, animals, shepherds, angels) with John's approach to the Christmas story.

references.name: Gospel of Matthew
references.context: Grouped with Luke as the only gospels containing the traditional nativity scene with barnyard animals and heavenly host.

references.name: Gospel of John (John 1:1-5)
references.context: The primary text for Christmas morning: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God'—a poetic, philosophical meditation on incarnation rather than narrative birth account.

references.name: Gospel of Mark
references.context: Referenced in passing as one of the four gospels, noted as earlier than John but lacking nativity narrative.

references.name: Historical scholarship on Gospel chronology
references.context: Preacher cites scholarly consensus that 'John was written last, well after Matthew, Mark, and Luke,' suggesting John may have known the synoptic nativity accounts and chosen an alternative interpretive lens.

references.name: Biblical hermeneutics / multiple interpretive lenses
references.context: The sermon's central framework: different gospels offer different 'lenses' on the same event—Matthew and Luke emphasizing particularity and narrative color, John emphasizing universality and theological meaning.

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: The theological doctrine that 'the Word became flesh,' central to John's prologue and to the sermon's meditation on the meaning of Christmas.

references.name: Being 'born again'
references.context: The preacher frames the Christmas invitation as spiritual rebirth through Christ, echoing Johannine theology (cf. John 3:3-7), emphasized especially for 'Episcopalians in the cathedral' who practice repeated spiritual renewal.

references.name: Universality vs. particularity in theology
references.context: A key theological distinction: Matthew and Luke present particular people (shepherds, angels, Mary, Joseph) moving toward the manger; John presents universal cosmic meaning—'all of us coming to birth in and through Christ.'

references.name: Sacramental theology / presence of the divine in all things
references.context: The recurring emphasis that 'the word echoes...just below the surface of every person we meet' and 'all people through this word and all living things are interconnected' reflects sacramental thinking about divine presence.

primary_theme: John's Gospel offers a theological and philosophical interpretation of incarnation focused on universal spiritual rebirth through the Word, contrasting with Matthew and Luke's narrative particularity of the nativity scene.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling interwoven with scholarly biblical analysis and pastoral invitation; the preacher uses a personal anecdote (the live nativity on Sand Mountain) as a concrete entry point to abstract theological reflection on gospel genre and hermeneutics, culminating in a spiritual call to renewal.

references.name: Ann Jones
references.context: Cathedral volunteer librarian who wrote 'Glory in the Wilderness' about the stained glass windows and identified the John the Baptist window as her least favorite.

references.name: Ann Willett
references.context: Designer of the John the Baptist stained glass window, described as having intentionally created a 'startling, unpleasant, unappealing' depiction.

references.name: Glory in the Wilderness
references.context: Book written by Ann Jones documenting the stained glass windows in the cathedral.

references.name: Matthew 11
references.context: Gospel passage where John the Baptist, imprisoned, sends followers to ask Jesus: 'Are you the one to come or should we wait for another?' — showing John's doubt and humility.

references.name: Matthew 3 (implied)
references.context: Passage containing John the Baptist's denunciation of Pharisees and Sadducees as 'brood of vipers' and his call to repentance.

references.name: Gospel accounts of John the Baptist
references.context: References to John's preaching of repentance, his baptism with water, and his testimony to Jesus as 'the one more powerful than I' who 'baptizes with water, Holy Spirit and fire.'

references.name: Episcopal theological tradition
references.context: Preacher identifies himself as an Episcopal priest and references decades of Episcopal teaching that 'repentance means to turn around, to reorient the whole of one's life, mind, body, and soul.'

references.name: Advent theology
references.context: The sermon is set during the Second Sunday of Advent and frames John's question as 'an Advent question' resonating with contemporary longing for peace, healing, and community.

primary_theme: John the Baptist as a prophetic voice calling for urgent repentance and moral clarity in Advent, whose confrontational demand for transformation contrasts with both his later doubt and modern preference for gentler spiritual guidance.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with prophetic challenge, using visual description of stained glass art as an entry point into theological meditation on biblical character, contemporary application, and the tension between comfort and truth-telling in religious life.

references.name: Jesus Christ (teachings and example)
references.context: Central to the sermon as the interpretive lens for understanding sainthood; Jesus's teaching 'do unto others as you would have them do unto you' and his example of love form the basis for understanding both 'all saints' and 'all souls.'

references.name: Desmond Tutu
references.context: Cited as a contemporary example of a saint who lived faithfully according to Jesus's teachings, fought apartheid in South Africa, advocated for human diversity, meditated on Christ's teachings, and reverenced Christ in the sacrament.

references.name: The Four Gospels (Matthew, Mark, Luke, John)
references.context: Referenced as the foundational source for understanding the tension between 'all saints' and 'all souls' and for examples of Jesus's inclusive approach to people on the margins.

references.name: Matthew 11:28-30
references.context: Quoted as the 'high point' of Jesus's second, broader approach: 'Come to me, all you who are weary and heavy laden, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you and learn from me for I'm gentle and lowly in heart and you will find rest for your souls. For my yoke is easy and my burden is light.'

references.name: Luke 17:11-19 (The Samaritan Leper)
references.context: Alluded to as an example of Jesus being drawn to marginalized people—specifically the Samaritan leper who was ostracized both for leprosy and for being Samaritan.

references.name: The Feeding Narratives (Gospel feeding miracles)
references.context: Referenced as examples of Jesus being drawn to crowds of hungry people who follow him for bread and life rather than specifically to hear his teachings.

references.name: Mustard seed faith
references.context: Alluded to when describing people Jesus is drawn to who have 'just a mustard seed of faith' (likely referencing Matthew 17:20 or Luke 17:6).

references.name: The Golden Rule
references.context: The phrase 'do unto others as you would have them do unto you' is presented as a summary of 'one of the high points of Jesus' teachings' and extended to apply to friends, neighbors, and even enemies.

references.name: The Anglican Communion / Episcopal Church
references.context: Referenced as the ecclesiastical tradition within which the sermon is preached; mentions the church's saints calendar and pastoral exception for moving All Saints Day to Sunday.

references.name: All Saints Day and All Souls Day liturgical tradition
references.context: The distinction between 'all saints' (those who lived faithfully and sacrificially) and 'all souls' (all people universally) is explored as a central theological and pastoral tension in Christian liturgy.

references.name: Requiem Mass tradition
references.context: Referenced as a decades-long practice in the community where the choir sings and names of the departed from the last 12 months are read on All Souls evening.

references.name: Sacramental theology (reverence for Christ in the sacrament)
references.context: Mentioned as a characteristic of Desmond Tutu's spirituality and by implication a mark of authentic sainthood in the Christian tradition.

primary_theme: The tension between understanding holiness as either exemplary faithfulness ('all saints') or universal belonging ('all souls') is resolved by Jesus's own inclusive practice and teaching, which calls both the devoted and the weary, the margin-dwellers and the faithful alike.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with narrative storytelling (opening pancake anecdote), scriptural exposition, contemporary example (Desmond Tutu), and liturgical-theological analysis, designed to comfort and invite rather than challenge.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: The entire sermon centers on Luke's gospel account of Zacchaeus (Luke 19:1-10) and uses Luke's theological themes throughout.

references.name: Luke 6:36 (Sermon on the Plain)
references.context: Preacher references Jesus's beatitude 'Blessed are the merciful, for they shall see God' from Luke's Sermon on the Plain, contrasting it with Matthew's Sermon on the Mount.

references.name: Gospel of Matthew
references.context: Sermon on the Mount is mentioned as the Matthean parallel to Luke's Sermon on the Plain, establishing Luke's distinctive theological emphasis on vision and seeing.

references.name: Luke 17:11-19 (Ten Lepers)
references.context: Preacher recounts a previous sermon on the healing of ten lepers to illustrate Luke's theme that 'seeing' in the gospel means spiritual perception and restored community.

references.name: St. Bernard of the Alps
references.context: Preacher mentions that St. Bernard is the official patron saint of climbers after looking up the patron saints for rock climbers on staff.

references.name: Early Christian theologians and mystics
references.context: Preacher references early theologians and mystics who called the spiritual perception of seeing 'the spiritual sense of the matter'—looking below the surface to see the soul and heart.

references.name: Christian mysticism / Spiritual vision tradition
references.context: The sermon develops a mystical theology where 'seeing' is equated with faith and spiritual perception, a core theme in Christian contemplative tradition.

references.name: Gothic/Neo-Gothic architectural theology
references.context: Preacher explains that vaulted ceilings in Gothic cathedrals deliberately mimic a forest canopy with tree trunks as columns, reflecting God's cosmos of justice, creativity, beauty, and mystery.

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: The sermon's emphasis on Jesus seeing and being present to peripheral characters reflects incarnational theology's focus on God's engagement with the particular and marginalized.

primary_theme: In Luke's gospel, 'seeing' is a form of faith that perceives spiritual truth and restores community; the story of Zacchaeus illustrates how Jesus sees those on the periphery and how we too must climb to new perspectives—spiritually and physically—to encounter divine truth.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling with pastoral reflection: The preacher uses a personal anecdote (William and Hudson climbing the oak tree) as a bridge to biblical interpretation, then develops theological themes through careful close reading of Lucan theology, culminating in an extended architectural metaphor that invites the congregation to physical and spiritual transformation.

references.name: Andrei Rublev
references.context: The sermon uses Rublev's icon of the Trinity as the visual and meditative focus for the three-week series 'Trinity in Motion.'

references.name: The Nicene Creed
references.context: Described as 'one of the great summarizations of the Christian faith' and serves as the structural framework for the entire sermon series and reflection.

references.name: Rowan Williams
references.context: Identified as 'the Welsh poet and former Archbishop of Canterbury,' quoted as saying the Creed represents 'statements about where I find the anchorage of my life, where I find solid ground, where I find home.'

references.name: Marion Mix
references.context: Described as a '20th century writer' who reframes the language of 'God the Father' as an invitation to 'come closer' rather than literal description.

references.name: Anglican and Episcopal tradition
references.context: Referenced as a theological tradition emphasizing that confidence, trust, refuge, and home 'find their expression through us, but have their genesis in God.'

references.name: Christian mysticism / apophatic theology
references.context: The concept of 'ineffability of the divine' and the acknowledgment that 'all of our language for and about God is an approximation' reflects apophatic theological tradition.

references.name: Good Shepherd Church (New York)
references.context: Credited with inspiring the Trinity in Motion series, mentioned as 'Andrew or Aaron Nyquist and the people of Good Shepherd in New York.'

primary_theme: Belief in God as grounded trust and relational anchorage rather than intellectual assent, using the Nicene Creed's affirmation of God the Father Almighty as an invitation to draw closer to divine goodness.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with contemplative mystical elements; seated teaching format inviting meditative prayer and wandering; uses trusted theological voices (Rowan Williams, Marion Mix) to reframe traditional credal language as relational rather than doctrinal.

references.name: Andrei Rublev
references.context: The sermon uses Rublev's icon of the Trinity as a visual focal point for prayer and reflection throughout the three-week series.

references.name: Nicene Creed
references.context: Described as 'one of the great summarizations of the Christian faith' and serves as the structural framework for the entire sermon series and reflection.

references.name: Rowan Williams
references.context: Quoted as 'the Welsh poet and former Archbishop of Canterbury' explaining that the Creed represents 'statements about where I find the anchorage of my life, where I find solid ground, where I find home.'

references.name: Marion Mix
references.context: A '20th century writer' cited to reframe the language of 'God the Father' not literally but as an invitation: 'come closer.'

references.name: Anglican and Episcopal tradition
references.context: The preacher identifies themselves within this tradition and notes how confidence, trust, refuge, and home are expressed through this ecclesiastical lineage.

references.name: Ineffability of the divine
references.context: The sermon emphasizes that 'no string of words, no piece of music, no series of statements adequately articulates the ineffability of the divine,' reflecting apophatic theology.

references.name: Apophatic theology
references.context: The sermon's insistence that all language about God is 'approximation' and cannot capture divine reality reflects the negative/apophatic theological tradition.

primary_theme: Belief in God the Father Almighty is not intellectual assent but a relational choice to find home, anchorage, and refuge in the divine Trinity, understood through approximation rather than literal language.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with scholarly framing; the preacher combines liturgical authority (Nicene Creed) with contemporary theological voices (Williams, Mix) to invite personal trust and contemplative prayer rather than doctrinal argument.

references.name: Jesus Christ
references.context: Gospel opening: 'follow me' - Jesus' words that frame the sermon's central call.

references.name: John 21 (Peter and the Beloved Disciple narrative)
references.context: The gospel reading for St. John's Day featuring Peter's misunderstanding about the beloved disciple's death.

references.name: St. Paul / Paul (apostle)
references.context: Referenced for the image that 'the body is a temple or a cathedral of the Holy Ghost' - contrasting stone cathedral with soul.

references.name: 1 Corinthians 6:19 (or similar Pauline body/temple passage)
references.context: Allusion to Paul's teaching that the body is a temple of the Holy Ghost; preacher acknowledges 'This is St. John's day, not St. Paul's day, but I will remind you of that incredible image from St. Paul.'

references.name: St. John the Evangelist (author of the Fourth Gospel)
references.context: The feast day celebrated; author of the Fourth Gospel after whom the cathedral is named; preacher explores theories of the beloved disciple's identity.

references.name: Gospel of John (Fourth Gospel)
references.context: Extensively referenced throughout; preacher notes 'we have a particular affinity, a particular love for the fourth gospel, its stories and themes.'

references.name: Roderick Greer
references.context: Named colleague from Episcopal church and school in Memphis, Tennessee where preacher previously worked.

references.name: Evan Zowsley
references.context: Named as cathedral's marketing communications director; described as 'brilliant' and 'an English major'; preacher notes she edits ideas but not sermons.

references.name: Mary Magdalene
references.context: One theory explored for the identity of the beloved disciple in John's gospel.

references.name: Lazarus
references.context: Another theory for the beloved disciple's identity; preacher notes 'makes a great deal of sense intellectually' given Jesus raised him from dead earlier in John's gospel.

references.name: Southern Baptist tradition
references.context: Preacher attended Southern Baptist Church in high school; recalls hymn 'I Have Decided to Follow Jesus' woven extemporaneously into the service.

references.name: Episcopal Church/Episcopal tradition
references.context: Preacher's denominational context; references Episcopal church in Memphis, Episcopal school, Episcopal cathedral; implicit theology of incarnational spirituality.

references.name: Medieval/contemplative spirituality (implicit)
references.context: The emphasis on 'looking up' in physical and spiritual life echoes contemplative traditions of raising the soul toward the divine through physical space and prayer.

references.name: Incarnational theology (implicit)
references.context: The core idea that 'our own body, our own stories, all that we are is a canopy... in which God is' reflects incarnational Christian theology.

references.name: "Pomp and Circumstance" (musical work)
references.context: The opening voluntary is titled 'Pomp and Circumstance'; preacher loves the music but not the title, preferring 'house of prayer' as the cathedral's image.

primary_theme: The cathedral and the human soul both function as dwelling places of God; spiritual awakening comes through learning to 'look up' in both physical sacred space and the midst of lived experience to perceive God's presence retrospectively.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection with autobiographical storytelling (girlfriend anecdote, Memphis ministry experience, parishioner visit), theological meditation on architectural symbolism, scholarly exploration of biblical identity theories, and invitation to personal spiritual practice.

references.name: Book of Ecclesiastes
references.context: The preacher extensively analyzes Ecclesiastes as a text expressing despair and futility ('everything is vanity,' 'chasing after the wind'), noting that rabbis and Christian theologians debated whether it should be included in the biblical canon.

references.name: Ecclesiastes 1:2 (or similar)
references.context: The phrase 'vanity of vanities' and the concept that 'everything is vanity' is quoted and discussed as the book's central claim.

references.name: Ecclesiastes 1:14 / 2:11 (or similar — 'chasing after the wind')
references.context: The preacher quotes the 'remarkable phrase' describing human effort as 'chasing after the wind' and develops an extended metaphor about shepherding the invisible wind.

references.name: Book of Revelation
references.context: Mentioned in passing as another controversial biblical text that Christian theologians debated including in the canon, parallel to objections against Ecclesiastes.

references.name: Book of Common Prayer
references.context: The preacher describes his early reliance on the Book of Common Prayer as the foundation of prayer life ('If you asked me then...what's the heart of a prayer life, I would have said, using this book') and his eventual learning to pray differently beyond it.

references.name: Canon Brodick
references.context: Cited as having preached on prayer the previous week with the key insight 'we are not God,' which the preacher uses to support his theme of humility.

references.name: Bishop of Alabama
references.context: Mentioned as the ecclesial authority who approved the preacher's entry into seminary, cited as a historical/narrative detail rather than intellectual authority.

references.name: Jewish rabbinic interpretation tradition
references.context: The preacher references rabbis' debate over the inclusion of Ecclesiastes in the canon, showing Jewish interpretive engagement with the text.

references.name: Christian theological tradition (medieval/Reformation)
references.context: Christian theologians' similar objections to Revelation's canonicity reflects broader Christian interpretive debates about troubling biblical texts.

references.name: Anglican/Episcopal theology and prayer tradition
references.context: The entire second half of the sermon engages with Anglican liturgical theology, the place of formal prayer (morning/evening prayer, Compline) in spiritual life, and the relationship between institutional liturgy and personal prayer.

references.name: Methodist tradition
references.context: The preacher mentions being 'raised in the Methodist church,' establishing his own denominational background before conversion to Episcopalianism.

references.name: Implicit reference: Eastern Orthodox apophatic theology or contemplative prayer traditions
references.context: The preacher's discovery that encountering God requires receptivity and immediacy ('just saying, wow, wow') rather than theological elaboration or textual meditation suggests an apophatic or contemplative approach to prayer.

references.name: Implicit reference: Protestant emphasis on sola scriptura or liturgical formalism
references.context: The preacher's initial belief that prayer 'wasn't there' without the Book of Common Prayer reflects a potential conflation of proper religious form with authentic spiritual experience.

primary_theme: Encountering God requires humility about the limits of human systems (theological, liturgical, or practical) and receptivity to God's presence beyond formal structures—a theme developed through analysis of Ecclesiastes's critique of human effort and the preacher's personal journey learning to pray authentically beyond the Book of Common Prayer.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative storytelling combined with biblical exegesis and personal testimony; the preacher moves from intellectual analysis of Ecclesiastes (scholarly approach) to extended autobiographical narrative (intimate, confessional tone) to ground abstract theological claims in lived experience.

references.name: The Four Gospels (Matthew, Mark, Luke, John)
references.context: The preacher notes that 'the Gospels, all four of them, preserve these stories about how ridiculous the apostles are,' highlighting that gospel writers didn't edit out the disciples' failures.

references.name: Luke 9:51-56 (Samaritan rejection narrative)
references.context: The primary gospel reading for the day, where James and John ask to call down fire on Samaritans who reject Jesus.

references.name: Galatians 5:16-26 (works of the flesh and fruit of the Spirit)
references.context: Paired reading contrasting the 'works of the flesh' (licentiousness, anger, jealousy, dissensions, factions) with the 'fruit of the Spirit' (joy, peace, kindness, gentleness, self-control).

references.name: Peter (Apostle)
references.context: Referenced as an example of the disciples' repeated failure to understand Jesus: 'Remember Peter, for example, about how many times they didn't get it.'

references.name: Assyrian deportation and Samaritan origins
references.context: Historical context explaining that Samaritans came from multiple nationalities brought by the Assyrians, creating ethnic and religious difference from Jerusalem-based Jews.

references.name: Jewish-Samaritan religious difference
references.context: The preacher notes that Samaritans had 'differences in terms of worship' compared to other Jews based in Jerusalem, establishing the backdrop of sectarian tension.

references.name: Episcopalian hermeneutical principle: 'take the Bible too seriously to take it literally'
references.context: Canon Broderick's aphorism that Episcopalians approach Scripture through the 'great arc of who God is' rather than proof-texting for political opinions.

references.name: Biblical proof-texting tradition (fundamentalist/literalist)
references.context: The preacher critiques 'this strand in the church' that 'use parts of the Bible, proof texts, to justify our own political opinions' to harm others.

references.name: Fundamentalist and literalist Christianity
references.context: Referenced as the source of pain experienced by LGBTQ Christians: 'the pain that many of these people experienced growing up in more fundamentalist or literalist churches.'

references.name: Episcopal Church liturgical tradition (Rite 1 and 'The Wilderness' service)
references.context: The preacher mentions the evening service called 'The Wilderness' as distinct from the 8 a.m. Rite 1 service, noting different music and prayers while maintaining 'beautiful Episcopal liturgy.'

references.name: Canon Broderick (contemporary theologian/homilist)
references.context: Quoted for the principle that 'Episcopalians take the Bible too seriously to take it literally' and credited with organizing LGBTQ testimonies during Pride Month at the cathedral.

references.name: Bishop Kim Lucas (Episcopal Bishop)
references.context: Referenced as leading Episcopalians in the Denver Pride parade alongside Methodist and Lutheran bishops, exemplifying inclusive Christian witness.

references.name: Jesus Christ (teachings and actions)
references.context: Presented as the embodiment of 'a God of love, a God of peace,' whose way contrasts with James and John's desire for destructive violence.

references.name: The Holy Spirit
references.context: Central to the Galatians passage, described as producing fruit (joy, peace, kindness, gentleness, self-control) against which 'there is no law.'

references.name: Christological hermeneutics (reading Scripture through Christ)
references.context: The preacher advocates looking to 'the great arc of who God is throughout the Scriptures' as revealed 'ultimately in Jesus Christ, in his teachings, in his actions.'

references.name: Ecclesiology of humility and self-correction
references.context: The sermon argues 'the church should be a humble church, a church that's always beginning again each morning,' drawing on the gospel writers' refusal to sanitize apostolic failure.

references.name: Liturgical year (Pentecost season)
references.context: The preacher explains that summer readings after Pentecost differ from Easter season readings, which 'all of them sort of have to do with overcoming death.'

primary_theme: Jesus' way of love and reconciliation offers an antidote to the church's tendency to use selective biblical interpretation to justify cruelty toward those perceived as different, as exemplified in the contrasting responses of James and John versus Jesus to the Samaritan rejection.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral-prophetic blend combining exegetical exposition, contemporary social witness (LGBTQ inclusion), personal anecdote (first parish sermon story), and doctrinal reflection on hermeneutics and the Holy Spirit. The preacher uses narrative scaffolding and lived examples to connect ancient Scripture to present ecclesial challenges.

references.name: Acts 2 (Pentecost narrative)
references.context: The foundation of the sermon; the preacher references the 'first reading' about the Spirit descending on disciples with tongues of flame and people speaking in other languages that are miraculously understood.

references.name: Peter's response in Acts 2:15
references.context: The preacher notes Peter's defense against accusations of drunkenness: 'It's too early for that... It's the 8 a.m. Rite One Eucharist.'

references.name: Willie James Jennings
references.context: The preacher cites Jennings as the author of 'After Whiteness' and paraphrases his statement that 'everybody has a soul.'

references.name: After Whiteness
references.context: A 'remarkable book' by Willie James Jennings cited near the sermon's conclusion to support the theological claim about universal human dignity and soul.

references.name: Episcopal Church tradition / Anglican liturgy
references.context: The preacher references the 'Rite One Eucharist,' the 41 provinces of the Episcopal Church worldwide, and the experience of liturgical worship as a unifying practice across languages.

references.name: Incarnational/Sacramental theology
references.context: The preacher emphasizes how 'ritual and actions' communicate 'on a level deeper than words' and how bread and wine become vehicles of blessing and gift-giving.

references.name: Pentecostal/Charismatic theology
references.context: The preacher invokes the Spirit's work as gathering people across differences and enabling mutual recognition beyond linguistic barriers.

primary_theme: The Holy Spirit's miraculous work at Pentecost demonstrates how God gathers and unites people across linguistic, cultural, and experiential differences by enabling deep human recognition and connection that transcends words.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling with pastoral reflection; the preacher uses extended personal anecdote (language immersion in Mexico) as the primary vehicle for theological insight, moving from concrete experience to universal spiritual principle.

references.name: Gospel of John
references.context: The entire sermon is built on John's gospel account of Jesus's departure and the promise of the Holy Spirit; preacher notes this is 'as remarkable, as significant as the Christmas story and even the Easter story.'

references.name: John 14 (implied passage on Jesus's departure and the Holy Spirit)
references.context: Jesus says 'I am going away' and 'where I'm going, you cannot come,' and promises the sending of the Holy Spirit as companion and advocate.

references.name: Christmas story (Gospel nativity accounts)
references.context: Used as a comparison point for the significance of the Pentecost/Spirit narrative in John's gospel.

references.name: Easter story (Gospel resurrection accounts)
references.context: Used as a comparison point for the significance of the Pentecost/Spirit narrative in John's gospel.

references.name: Book of Revelation (John of Patmos)
references.context: Second reading of the liturgical day; John of Patmos is led by the Spirit up a high mountain to receive visions, contrasted with the quieter Spirit of John's gospel.

references.name: Church of England priest and gospel scholar (unnamed)
references.context: Cited as having written an outstanding commentary on John's gospel; this scholar notes that in John, the Holy Spirit is not primarily associated with 'extraordinary manifestations like dreams and visions and miracles and prophecy' but with 'the ordinary, the day-by-day, the long-haul.'

references.name: Sacramental theology / Traditional sacramental definition
references.context: Preacher invokes the classical definition: 'sacraments are outward and visible signs of inward and spiritual grace,' and argues this is why Episcopalians encounter the Holy Spirit primarily through ordinary material practice rather than extraordinary visions.

references.name: Episcopalian theological tradition
references.context: Preacher characterizes Episcopalians as valuing 'reason as much as we enjoy revelation' and 'subtlety as much as we enjoy the obvious,' and as trusting the Holy Spirit through sacramental experience rather than ecstatic phenomena.

references.name: 'God's frozen chosen' (Episcopalian stereotype)
references.context: Preacher acknowledges this unfair characterization of Episcopalians as reserved or unemotional, but defends it as reflecting a genuine theological emphasis on reason, subtlety, and sacramental rather than charismatic experience.

primary_theme: The Holy Spirit, as John's gospel presents it, is an invisible but genuinely present companion, advocate, and guide who meets believers in the ordinary, material events of daily life rather than in extraordinary visions, and this understanding particularly resonates with Episcopal theology's emphasis on sacramental experience.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling combined with pastoral reflection and scholarly interpretation; the preacher opens with a memoir from Memphis to establish the problem (the invisibility of God), then moves to close textual analysis of John's gospel, cites scholarly commentary, and concludes with theological reflection on sacramental practice suited to Episcopalian sensibilities.

references.name: Gospel of John
references.context: The preacher repeatedly references John's gospel account of the crucifixion and resurrection, noting that John frames the crucifixion as an execution scene and emphasizes Easter even while telling the Good Friday story.

references.name: The Nicene Creed
references.context: Preacher references the Creed's mention of Pontius Pilate as the historical agent of Christ's execution.

references.name: Matthew, Mark, and Luke Gospels
references.context: The preacher compares John's account of Mary Magdalene going to the tomb 'while it was still dark' with Matthew, Mark, and Luke's versions where the visit occurs at dawn.

references.name: Good Friday and Easter narrative (Four Gospels)
references.context: The entire sermon structure is built on the Gospel accounts of Christ's crucifixion on Good Friday and resurrection on Easter Sunday, particularly the appearance to the disciples where Christ says 'Peace be with you.'

references.name: St. Paul
references.context: The preacher compares Canon Katie to 'St. Paul on staff' because of her practice of letter-writing, evoking the apostle's epistolary ministry.

references.name: The Creed (Apostolic/Nicene tradition)
references.context: Referenced when mentioning Pontius Pilate's inclusion in the Creed as the historical agent of the crucifixion.

references.name: Resurrection theology / Eschatological hope
references.context: The sermon engages Christian resurrection doctrine and eschatological promises that violence will not have the last word and that Easter represents God's future breaking into the present.

references.name: Peace theology / Nonviolence
references.context: The sermon emphasizes Christ's peace and nonviolence as central to the resurrection message, contrasting with expected divine retaliation or military power.

references.name: Canon Katie (sermon reference)
references.context: The preacher mentions a previous sermon by 'Ken and Broderick' (Canon Broderick) on Good Friday that contained the line 'God is turning an instrument of death into a signpost of life,' which the preacher uses to illustrate the pairing of Good Friday and Easter.

references.name: Implicit Bonhoefferian theme (costly grace vs. cheap grace / discipleship under the cross)
references.context: The sermon's emphasis on remembering Good Friday before celebrating Easter, and the call to follow Christ in self-sacrificial peace rather than seeking power, echoes themes of costly discipleship.

primary_theme: Christ's resurrection on Easter brings a peace rooted in self-sacrificial love and nonviolence, not in divine power or retaliation, and this promise sustains hope and healing even amid contemporary violence and grief.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection with prophetic challenge; the preacher uses imaginative empathy (imagining himself as Pontius Pilate's carpenter), comparative exegesis of gospel accounts, personal testimony (the letter-writing story), and contemporary application (Ukraine, 2022) to invite listeners into deeper understanding of Easter's meaning.

references.name: Book of Common Prayer
references.context: Referenced as the official prayer book of the Episcopal Church used by the congregation.

references.name: Book of Occasional Services
references.context: Cited as the official clergy resource containing blessings and services not regularly used, including the blessing of church furniture and the concept of consecration through use.

references.name: Luke 13:1-5 (Gospel reading for Third Sunday in Lent)
references.context: The primary biblical text discussed, featuring Jesus addressing why bad things happen to people, with references to Pilate mixing Galilean blood with sacrifices and a tower falling killing 18 people.

references.name: Jesus' 40-day wilderness journey
references.context: Referenced as the biblical parallel that Lent mirrors, establishing Jesus as a companion on the journey through suffering and hard questions.

references.name: Jesus' baptism
references.context: Mentioned as the event after which Jesus undertook his 40-day wilderness journey, which Lenten practice imitates.

references.name: The Cross and Resurrection
references.context: Referenced as providing a 'wordless answer' to theodicy questions beyond Jesus' explicit teaching, through the pattern of his life and death.

references.name: Washington National Cathedral
references.context: Mentioned as a major ecclesiastical work designed by architect Terry Eason, establishing his credibility.

references.name: Venerable tradition (of consecration through use)
references.context: Quoted from the Book of Occasional Services as an ancient Christian practice of consecrating church furnishings through their liturgical use rather than formal blessing alone.

references.name: Cathedral nave usage in England
references.context: Referenced as a historical precedent showing cathedrals have been flexibly used for hospitals, shelter, and other community purposes beyond worship.

references.name: Episcopal Church theology/practice
references.context: Implicitly invoked throughout the sermon's liturgical framework, understanding of confirmation, baptism, and the church calendar.

references.name: Pastoral accompaniment theology
references.context: The theological claim that Jesus functions primarily as a companion listener rather than an answer-giver reflects modern pastoral and relational theology.

references.name: Theodicy (problem of evil)
references.context: The central intellectual question addressed: why evil and suffering exist if God is good and loving, framed through both youth and adult confirmation classes.

primary_theme: In the midst of Lent's wilderness journey, Jesus functions not primarily as a teacher offering answers to the problem of evil and suffering, but as a compassionate companion who listens and walks alongside us through life's hardest questions.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection combining liturgical commentary, youth engagement anecdotes, scriptural exegesis, and relational theology; the preacher builds intimacy through concrete examples and invites emotional identification rather than scholarly argumentation.

references.name: Luke's Gospel
references.context: Luke's account of Jesus' baptism and the Spirit driving Jesus into the wilderness is the primary text examined throughout the sermon.

references.name: Matthew 4:1-11 / Luke 4:1-13 (Temptation narrative)
references.context: The three temptations of Jesus in the wilderness, particularly the third temptation where Jesus is offered the ability to throw himself off the temple pinnacle.

references.name: The Lord's Prayer
references.context: The petition 'Deliver us from evil' is cited in the context of Christians praying in Kyiv during Russian bombardment.

references.name: Pamela Cooper-White
references.context: Episcopal priest with a PhD in psychology who teaches at Union Seminary; cited as author of scholarly work on evil and psychology.

references.name: Braided Selves
references.context: Academic book by Pamela Cooper-White containing a chapter on evil that defines evil as what tears the fabric of human relationships and community.

references.name: Christian iconography and art history
references.context: The preacher references scholarly work on the history of Christian iconography, noting that Satan did not appear in Christian artistic depictions until the ninth century.

references.name: Psychological/psychic framework
references.context: The sermon uses psychological terminology (intra-psychic, demythologize) to interpret Satan as internal voices rather than external forces.

references.name: Unnamed Episcopal bishop of Diocese of Alabama
references.context: Cited as authority for the wisdom that 'if you spot it, you got it'—a psychological insight about projection.

references.name: Church of England / Anglican tradition
references.context: Reference to the Diocese of Europe, parishes in Moscow and Kyiv, and the Lord's Prayer petition demonstrates liturgical and ecclesiastical framework.

references.name: The 40 days and 40 nights
references.context: Biblical reference to Jesus' duration in the wilderness, used as comparison for extended periods of inner wrestling.

references.name: Demythologization
references.context: The preacher explicitly uses the term 'demythologize' to describe reinterpreting Satan as internal psychological forces rather than supernatural entities.

references.name: Religious temptation to confuse faith and magic
references.context: The third temptation is interpreted as the perennial religious temptation to believe faith makes us superheroic or that prayer fixes things instantly.

references.name: White supremacy ideology
references.context: Cited as contemporary example of evil that is societal, destructive, and paired with violence at its most destructive level.

references.name: Theology of divine presence (Christology)
references.context: The sermon concludes with affirmation that Christ 'promises to be with us always' in solitude and community, emphasizing incarnational theology.

references.name: Theology of community and communion
references.context: The preacher emphasizes that keeping company with one another and gathering in community is 'the most comforting and urgent and holy thing' we can do.

primary_theme: In the wilderness temptations, Jesus wrestles with internal and external forces of evil, demonstrating his solidarity with human struggle; Christians are comforted knowing they are never alone in their moral wrestling because Christ relates to them, and community gathered in prayer offers protection and solidarity against destructive evil.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with scholarly apparatus: the preacher combines accessible spiritual insight with academic references (iconography history, psychological theory, theological scholarship), uses contemporary events (Ukrainian crisis) to ground abstract theology, employs personal anecdote (the bishop's wisdom), and moves from intellectual analysis to affective and prophetic conclusion emphasizing community.

references.name: The Golden Rule
references.context: "Do unto others as you would have them do unto you" - cited as a key teaching about love.

references.name: Matthew 5:46-47 (or Luke 6:32-36)
references.context: Jesus' question: "If you only love people who love you back, what good is that?" - paraphrased as the most difficult part of the teaching.

references.name: Luke 6:38
references.context: The image of love returning to us "pressed down in our laps" from the Most High - referenced as a closing image of the Gospel reading.

references.name: The Crucifixion / The Cross
references.context: Jesus loved those who did not love him back, and "it cost him his life" - presented as commentary on Jesus' teaching about love.

references.name: The Sanctus
references.context: Referenced as "one of the high points in the Eucharistic prayer" during the children's time.

references.name: The Collect (Collects for the Day)
references.context: The opening prayer is cited as expressing the day's teaching succinctly and poetically, praising God for pouring the Holy Spirit and giving the gift of love.

references.name: Episcopal/Christian Prayer Practice
references.context: For Episcopalians and Christians, love is paired with prayer; thanksgiving for acts of love and justice, and continuous petition for more.

references.name: The Most High / God as Love
references.context: Theological framework throughout: God as the source of love, kindness, tenderness, and continuous giving; becoming 'children of the Most High' and 'children of the One who is love.'

primary_theme: Love is always paired with specific gifts, virtues, and practices (healing, wisdom, humor, justice, prayer), and the deepest form of Christian love extends to those who do not love us back, mirroring Christ's self-giving.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection using concrete parish examples (Dr. Lise Barber, Fran Trujillo, a three-year-old child) to illustrate theological principles, followed by scriptural and liturgical grounding.

references.name: Isaiah (the prophet)
references.context: Isaiah 6 - the foundational text where Isaiah experiences a mystical vision of God in the temple in the year of King Uzziah's death, seeing six-winged seraphim praising God.

references.name: Isaiah 6:1-4
references.context: The vision of the seraphim with six wings, veiling their faces and feet, flying about, and shouting 'Holy, holy, holy is the Lord of heavenly forces.'

references.name: Sarah, Rebecca, and Rachel
references.context: Named as the matriarchs whose God Isaiah encounters in his vision, representing continuity with Israel's covenant tradition.

references.name: Eastern Orthodox tradition
references.context: The preacher references the Eastern Orthodox understanding that the Trisagion ('Holy, holy, holy') is sung constantly by the seraphim in God's presence and that congregational singing joins this eternal chorus.

references.name: Seraphim
references.context: The six-winged angelic creatures from Isaiah 6, invoked throughout to represent God's otherness and the overwhelming nature of divine encounter.

references.name: Liturgical protocol / proper worship decorum
references.context: The preacher contrasts the loud, exuberant shouting of the seraphim with restrained liturgical practices, humorously noting 'The seraphim are not Episcopalians,' to challenge assumptions about dignified worship.

references.name: Mysticism / Mystical vision
references.context: The sermon frames Isaiah's experience as a 'mystical vision' and discusses how human beings are 'not wired to experience God's full glory all at once,' drawing on mystical theology's understanding of divine encounter.

references.name: Paradox (expectation and surprise)
references.context: The preacher identifies the paradoxical nature of 'expecting to be surprised' as a spiritual practice, noting 'That's paradoxical' and wrestling with the logical problem this poses.

references.name: Biblical hermeneutics - the significance of 'But'
references.context: The preacher notes that in the Bible, when an author says 'But,' it signals a turning point: 'And in the Bible, when an author says, but, it's time to perk up and listen.'

primary_theme: God transcends human containment and expectation; encountering God's full glory requires openness and expansiveness of imagination rather than attempts to domesticate the divine into manageable terms.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with contemporary cultural examples, prophetic challenge to small expectations, mystical theology, and humorous social commentary (especially regarding liturgical formality). The preacher uses personal anecdote, congregational address, paradox, and prayer as rhetorical modes.

references.name: Apostle Paul
references.context: Paul is analyzed as a missionary mind translating Jewish Christian stories into meaning for Greek gentiles who lack presuppositions about the God of Abraham; his refusal to tell Gospel stories is interrogated as puzzling and pedagogically significant.

references.name: 1 Corinthians (entire epistle)
references.context: The preacher mentions conducting a year-long Bible study on 1 Corinthians and notes Paul's absence of Jesus narratives throughout the epistle.

references.name: 1 Corinthians 13
references.context: Paul's 'poetic and so powerful and succinct' passage on love ('Love is patient, love is kind...') is quoted and treated as the culmination of Paul's theological meaning-making and as the church's mission statement.

references.name: Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John (Gospels)
references.context: Contrasted with Paul's epistolary approach; the Gospels are identified as narrative-based, containing stories of healing (woman's daughter), feeding (5,000), resurrection, and Jesus eating with people.

references.name: Julian of Norwich
references.context: Quoted for the phrase 'love was his meaning,' applied to Paul's vision and the church's mission.

references.name: The Cross and Resurrection (Pauline theology)
references.context: Paul 'talks a lot about the cross, even more, thankfully, about the resurrection and what it all means,' indicating centrality of crucifixion and resurrection theology in his missionary work.

references.name: Jesus (Matthew 19:14 or parallels)
references.context: 'Let the little children come to me, Jesus said'—invoked to affirm the church's renewed commitment to children's formation ministry.

references.name: The Body of Christ (Pauline ecclesiology)
references.context: Paul is described as 'writing this passage about what it means to be the body of Christ, what it means to be the church, what it means to be the Christian community,' establishing 1 Corinthians 13 as a vision statement for communal identity.

references.name: Episcopal Church theology and polity
references.context: References to 'the Episcopal Church in Colorado,' diocesan services, the office of the bishop, and 'this wonderful sense of Episcopal community' situate the sermon within Anglican ecclesiology and institutional practice.

references.name: God of Abraham and Sarah
references.context: Used as shorthand for Jewish theological presuppositions that Paul's Corinthian audience lacked, illustrating the cultural translation problem Paul faced.

primary_theme: Love (1 Corinthians 13) is the church's mission, vision, and culture; generous pledging enables the community to restore full-time ministries and expand love-centered pastoral care.
rhetorical_style: Scholarly-pastoral hybrid: opens with biblical-critical observation (Paul's narrative silence), transitions to theological exegesis and mystical appeal (Julian of Norwich), then shifts to administrative-prophetic reflection connecting love theology to budgetary decisions and staff expansion, employing lived example and institutional transparency as moral argument.

references.name: Carl Jung
references.context: Jung identified as a great 20th-century psychologist whose theory of synchronicity is introduced as a lens for understanding meaningful coincidence and non-linear time.

references.name: Jung's Theory of Synchronicity
references.context: Defined as 'a meaningful coincidence of two or more events where something other than the probability of chance is involved,' used to reframe understanding of time as non-linear and pregnant with divine meaning.

references.name: Kronos (Greek god of time)
references.context: Referenced when explaining that 'time is chronology' and 'time is the Greek god Kronos,' contrasting linear time with Paul's concept of 'fullness of time.'

references.name: St. Paul (Apostle Paul)
references.context: Cited as the primary biblical authority for the sermon's interpretation of Christmas through his phrase 'in the fullness of time' and his understanding of the Spirit's indwelling presence.

references.name: Galatians 4:4-6
references.context: Paul's statement 'God sent his son born of a human mother' and 'God sends the Spirit into your hearts' and 'the Spirit cries Abba, Father' forms the core text of the sermon.

references.name: Genesis 1:2 (Creation narrative)
references.context: Alluded to when the preacher notes that 'the Spirit is the same Spirit that hovered over the waters of creation in the beginning.'

references.name: Book of Revelation
references.context: Referenced in contrast to Paul's understanding of the Spirit as continuous indwelling rather than one-time visions 'like it happens in the book of Revelation.'

references.name: Pentecost narrative
references.context: The preacher observes that 'Paul's Christmas sermon really sounds more like a Pentecost sermon,' connecting the Spirit's gift at Christmas to the Spirit's role at Pentecost.

references.name: Paul Holloway
references.context: New Testament scholar and Pauline scholar teaching at the University of the South (Sewanee) cited for his interpretation that the Spirit dwells continuously in believers rather than appearing only in visions.

references.name: Medieval/patristic spirituality on non-linear time
references.context: Referenced when the preacher notes this view was 'shared by almost all of our spiritual ancestors for centuries, that time is not linear.'

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: Implicit throughout the sermon's emphasis on God becoming human 'so close to us that we cradle that child' and the Spirit indwelling ordinary human life.

primary_theme: In the fullness of time, God reveals himself through Christ and indwells human hearts with the Spirit, calling believers to recognize moments pregnant with divine meaning and to pay attention to God's continuous, intimate presence in their ordinary lives.
rhetorical_style: Scholarly pastoral reflection combining intellectual analysis (Jung, Paul, textual exegesis) with contemplative, poetic language; balances academic precision with spiritual encouragement; uses layered metaphors (pregnancy, fullness, cradle) to make abstract theology intimate and accessible.

references.name: Rowan Williams
references.context: Archbishop of Canterbury, described as 'probably the most brilliant theologian to be the Archbishop of Canterbury in the last five or six hundred years,' who responded to a child's letter about God's invention with theological wisdom.

references.name: John's Gospel
references.context: Cited as the source of the Christmas narrative beginning with 'In the beginning was the Word,' emphasizing God's pre-existence and creative communication rather than birth narratives.

references.name: John 1:1
references.context: Direct quote: 'In the beginning was the Word. And the Word was with God, and the Word was God.'

references.name: John 1:14
references.context: Quoted: 'And the Word was made flesh and dwelt among us.'

references.name: Luke's Gospel
references.context: Cited as the source of the traditional Christmas narrative with pathos, details, and color, contrasted with John's more abstract approach.

references.name: Luke 2:7
references.context: Quoted multiple times: 'Mary gave birth to her firstborn son and wrapped him in bands of cloth and laid him in a manger for there was no place for them in the inn.'

references.name: Naomi Washington Leaphard
references.context: Director of Philadelphia's Department of Interfaith and Faith Communities, cited for her insight about the tragic dimension of the Christmas story—Jesus born homeless—and its moral implications for contemporary society.

references.name: The incarnation
references.context: Theological concept invoked to understand God's presence with the homeless and dispossessed, beginning with Jesus born without shelter.

references.name: Anglican Communion / Episcopal Church
references.context: Referenced as the ecclesiastical context for understanding Rowan Williams' authority and the sermon's cathedral setting.

references.name: Christmas pageant tradition
references.context: Invoked to illustrate the difference between John's abstract theology and Luke's narrative-based, dramatizable Christmas story.

references.name: God's self-communication
references.context: Implicit theological theme drawn from John's Logos theology: God as eternally free, living, creative, and self-communicative.

references.name: Divine surprise/encountering the unexpected divine
references.context: Emphasis on how people 'discover' God and 'were quite surprised'—a mystical tradition of encountering the divine through wonder and the unexpected.

primary_theme: God was not invented by human beings or the church, but reveals himself through creation and history; the Christmas story in Luke reveals God's identification with the homeless and dispossessed, challenging comfortable Christian assumptions.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling combined with pastoral reflection and prophetic challenge; uses an anecdote (Lulu's letter) to introduce theological depth, juxtaposes John's abstract cosmology with Luke's concrete social witness, and ends with a prophetic call to recognize Jesus in the homeless.

references.name: David Brooks
references.context: Preacher cites Brooks's recent Atlantic article on nuclear families and the importance of extended families as 'shock absorbers' for children in intense family systems.

references.name: David Brooks, 'The Atlantic' article on nuclear family
references.context: Preacher references a specific article by David Brooks in The Atlantic about extended families, searchable as 'David Brooks Atlantic nuclear family.'

references.name: Gospel of Luke / Luke's account of the Visitation
references.context: The sermon's primary scriptural foundation: the visitation of Mary and Elizabeth as told in Luke's gospel, formally known as the Visitation narrative.

references.name: Luke 1:26-38 (Annunciation)
references.context: Angel Gabriel announces to Mary that she will conceive and bear a child who is the savior of the world; Mary responds with theological questioning ('How can this be?').

references.name: Luke 1:46-55 (Magnificat / Song of Mary)
references.context: Preacher extensively interprets Mary's song ('My soul magnifies the Lord, my spirit rejoices in God my Savior') as expressing her theological and poetic imagination, with connections to Jesus's later teachings.

references.name: The Beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-10 or Luke 6:20-23)
references.context: Preacher draws a 'straight line' from Mary's song to Jesus's Beatitudes ('Blessed are the poor,' 'Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness,' 'Blessed are the meek and lowly'), arguing Jesus learned this prophetic vision from his mother.

references.name: John the Baptist's witness to Jesus
references.context: Preacher references John the Baptist's deferential statements about Jesus, including 'Jesus must increase, I must decrease' and 'I baptize with water, He baptizes with water and fire.'

references.name: Angel Gabriel
references.context: Gabriel announces to Mary that Elizabeth is her relative and that Mary will conceive; the preacher notes Gabriel's careful theological response to Mary's questioning.

references.name: Christian liturgical tradition / Song of Mary in worship
references.context: Preacher references the Magnificat as having been sung liturgically between the two lessons in the Advent/Christmas Eve service.

references.name: Advent and Christmas theology
references.context: The sermon situates the Visitation narrative within Advent preparation and Christmas Eve expectation, framing Christmas as a continuation of Mary and Elizabeth's meeting.

references.name: Extended family as theological/social concept
references.context: Brooks's framework of extended family members (cousins, aunts, uncles, grandparents) serving as 'shock absorbers' echoes biblical and theological understandings of kinship as a site of spiritual formation and alternative witness.

references.name: Prophetic imagination
references.context: Preacher repeatedly emphasizes Mary's 'theological imagination' and 'prophetic imagination' in her song, invoking a tradition of understanding prophecy as imaginative moral vision rather than mere prediction.

primary_theme: The meeting of Mary and Elizabeth demonstrates how extended familial and relational networks—particularly those with different perspectives and experiences—provide crucial spiritual support and prophetic witness; this Visitation narrative prefigures Christmas as a community gathering centered on expectation of Christ's liberating presence.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection combined with scholarly literary analysis; the preacher moves from personal anecdote (conversation with parish priest about family dysfunction) through contemporary intellectual sources (David Brooks) to biblical exegesis, inviting imaginative engagement with scripture while connecting theological themes to listeners' lived experience of family and preparation.

references.name: John's Gospel
references.context: The preacher uses John's gospel as the primary scriptural foundation, noting that the cathedral is named after St. John the Evangelist and the day's gospel reading is from John.

references.name: John 10 (The Good Shepherd parable)
references.context: 'The shepherd calls the sheep by name and leads them out' — cited as the chapter immediately before the Lazarus narrative to establish the theme of naming and recognition.

references.name: John 11 (Lazarus narrative)
references.context: The raising of Lazarus is used as the primary narrative example of how Jesus knows people by name and grieves for individuals, including the verse 'Jesus wept.'

references.name: John 11:35 ('Jesus wept')
references.context: Identified as 'the shortest verse in English in the Bible' and used to illustrate Jesus's intimate knowledge of and emotional connection to Lazarus.

references.name: John 20 (Mary Magdalene at the resurrection)
references.context: Mary Magdalene recognizes the risen Jesus when he speaks her name, 'Mary,' demonstrating how naming and recognition are essential to resurrection in John's theology.

references.name: Godly Play
references.context: Referenced as the elementary school program that uses the Good Shepherd parable and teaches children through wondering questions like 'I wonder if the good shepherd knows your name.'

references.name: All Saints' Sunday
references.context: The liturgical occasion that frames the sermon's reflection on naming, recognition, and the communion of saints across time.

references.name: Baptism
references.context: Invoked as the Christian practice in which names are called out and individuals are marked as 'Christ's own forever,' embodying the principle of naming and belonging central to the sermon.

references.name: The communion of saints
references.context: Referenced when the preacher describes the community mingling 'our prayers with those who've come before us and mingle our prayers with those who will surely follow us.'

references.name: Resurrection theology in John's gospel
references.context: The preacher emphasizes that in John's gospel, resurrection is fundamentally about recognition and belonging, not merely physical restoration.

primary_theme: In John's gospel, God's knowledge and calling of people by name—illustrated through the Lazarus narrative and Mary Magdalene's recognition of the risen Christ—establishes that true resurrection and redemption are about recognition, belonging, and intimate relationship, not abstract theology.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative storytelling interwoven with scriptural exegesis; the preacher uses a personal childhood anecdote (the kickball story) as a theological teaching tool to make abstract spiritual truths emotionally resonant and existentially relevant to the congregation.

references.name: Annie Dillard
references.context: Preacher cites Dillard as beloved author and uses her work extensively to frame theological reflection on sight and perception.

references.name: Pilgrim at Tinker Creek
references.context: Pulitzer Prize-winning work by Dillard (1970s) containing the chapter 'Seeing' about cataract surgery and restored sight.

references.name: Teaching a Stone to Talk
references.context: Dillard work recommended as alternative entry point to her writing alongside her autobiography.

references.name: Dillard's autobiography
references.context: Mentioned as recommended reading to introduce oneself to Dillard's work.

references.name: Mark's Gospel (Blind Bartimaeus story)
references.context: Central biblical narrative (Mark 10:46-52) about blind Bartimaeus regaining sight, used as lens for theological reflection.

references.name: John's Gospel (man born blind)
