references.context: Preacher references the glory of the Lord appearing as 'a devouring fire' on Mount Sinai as parallel to Jesus' transfigured light.

references.name: Matthew 17:9 (Command to silence)
references.context: Jesus tells the three disciples not to speak of the transfiguration 'until after the Son of Man has been raised from the dead.'

references.name: Matthew 28:20 (Resurrection promise)
references.context: Referenced through the phrase 'I will be with you always...even to the end of the ages.'

references.name: John 11:35 (Jesus weeping at Lazarus's death)
references.context: Alluded to when preacher says 'in his grief over the death of Lazarus his face is shining.'

references.name: Gospel healing narratives (general)
references.context: Preacher references healings, especially of children and those who suffer, as moments when 'Jesus' face is shining.'

references.name: Gospel feeding miracles
references.context: Cited as an example of where divine light/presence is manifest: 'In the feeding of the thousands, His clothes are dazzling white.'

references.name: Gospel parables
references.context: Referenced as stories about 'the heavenly being found in the commonplace, in the midst of our own stories.'

references.name: Twelve-step recovery program (AA)
references.context: Preacher describes Wesley's recovery from alcoholism using language of 'putting one step in front of others, following those steps day by day, trusting in a higher power.'

references.name: Jacob wrestling the angel/divine encounter
references.context: The phrase 'wrestled with demons and danced with God' echoes the biblical motif of wrestling with the divine (Genesis 32:24-30), used to describe Wesley's spiritual struggle.

references.name: Light as symbol of divinity
references.context: Preacher establishes that 'light is, of course, a symbol of divinity' and explores how light represents the heavenly and enlightening in Christian tradition.

references.name: Mystical theology of divine presence in ordinary life
references.context: Central to the sermon's argument that transfiguration light 'is not something separate from our very ordinary stories' but shines upon 'our own living...our own dying...our own rising.'

references.name: Unconditional love as divine promise
references.context: Preacher frames 'Do not be afraid' as 'the flip side, the shadow side of Jesus telling us that he loves us unconditionally and eternally.'

primary_theme: The transfiguration reveals that divine light is not confined to mountaintop experiences but continuously shines through ordinary human stories of struggle, recovery, and faithful presence, inviting us to recognize Christ's face in ourselves and our neighbors.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection combining personal anecdote (Wesley Emerson's memorial service) with scriptural interpretation and theological meditation, building from cosmic theology to intimate spiritual insight.

references.name: Charles Connick
references.context: Stained glass window designer whose love of blue color and its theological significance is explored throughout the sermon as a parallel to the feast day's theme of light.

references.name: Adventures in Light and Color
references.context: Charles Connick's book, cited as the source of his reflections on how light reveals color and his theological understanding of blue.

references.name: Book of Exodus 24
references.context: Referenced for the image of the Lord God Almighty walking on a pavement of sapphire that matches the clarity of the heavens, a favorite Bible verse of Connick's.

references.name: Carl Jung
references.context: Attributed as the originator of the quote 'the brighter the light, the darker the shadow,' which becomes the sermon's central interpretive lens.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: The primary scriptural source for the Presentation narrative (Luke 2), providing the core text for the sermon.

references.name: Song of Simeon (Nunc Dimittis)
references.context: Referenced as the liturgical song traditionally sung on Candlemas, in which Simeon blesses Jesus as 'the light that will enlighten God.'

references.name: Simeon's Prophecy
references.context: The prophecy in Luke 2:34-35 about the child being 'a sign of the rising and falling of Israel' and 'a sword will pierce your own heart,' interpreted as foreshadowing the Passion.

references.name: Letter to the Hebrews
references.context: Referenced in the second service reading with the phrase that 'Christ did not come to help the angels,' which the preacher notes he had never previously contemplated.

references.name: Baptismal Covenant
references.context: The Episcopal baptismal covenant's language about striving 'for justice and peace' is cited as part of the spiritual life Christians attempt to live.

references.name: Jewish Law
references.context: Referenced as the legal backdrop for the presentation and purification rites that Jesus and Mary undergo in the temple narrative.

references.name: Candlemas
references.context: The alternate name for the Feast of the Presentation, explained as stemming from Simeon's reference to Christ as light and traditionally celebrated with lit candles.

references.name: Blues musicians and the blues tradition
references.context: The preacher notes that Connick overlooked blues musicians when writing about prophets, priests, and poets using the color blue, suggesting this as a gap in Connick's 1930s perspective.

references.name: Jungian psychology and shadow work
references.context: Implicit invocation through the Jung quote; the sermon uses the psychological concept of shadow (the darker aspects produced by light) as a lens for interpreting the Presentation narrative.

primary_theme: The Feast of the Presentation celebrates Christ as light entering the world, but this light necessarily casts shadows—both in the foreshadowing of Christ's Passion and in the spiritual struggles of Christian life—reminding us that following Christ involves both illumination and difficulty.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative with aesthetic observation; the preacher weaves together architectural and artistic description (stained glass windows), theological reflection, personal anecdote (the job interview with the psychiatrist), and scriptural interpretation to build a meditation on light and shadow as spiritual realities.

references.name: Biblical lessons and liturgical seasons (Advent)
references.context: Preacher notes that normally he begins sermon preparation by looking at biblical lessons or considering the liturgical season, such as Advent.

references.name: Annual vestry election and parish governance
references.context: The sermon functions as both a preaching event and the annual Dean's Report, combining two institutional practices into one.

references.name: Stewardship as spiritual practice
references.context: Financial giving is framed as 'a spiritual practice' and part of 'offering all that we are and all that we have at our Lord's altar,' drawing on Christian stewardship theology.

references.name: Living within one's means as spiritual practice
references.context: The cathedral's financial approach is described as a spiritual practice: 'we live within our means and we believe that too is a spiritual practice.'

references.name: Compassion and wisdom (Christian virtue tradition)
references.context: The preacher states that 'what St. John's Cathedral always wants to do is to be compassionate and wise' in response to homelessness on cathedral grounds.

primary_theme: The Dean's annual report delivered as a sermon, addressing immediate pastoral concerns (homelessness outreach and community partnerships) and financial health, while emphasizing spiritual practices of giving, stewardship, and wisdom.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with narrative storytelling; the preacher uses specific examples (St. Francis Center, individual staff members like Kathy Carfray and Fran Trujillo, the 14 people camping on cathedral grounds) to illustrate theological principles. The tone is conversational and encouraging, mixing institutional reporting with spiritual exhortation.

references.name: Sam Pitaro
references.context: Episcopal priest friend cited to explain the chronological gaps in the gospels using the analogy of a family photo album with missing photos over months and years.

references.name: The Four Gospels
references.context: Referenced as containing mysterious gaps in Christ's life narrative, particularly the 20-30 year gap between infancy and baptism.

references.name: Matthew 3:13-17 (or parallel baptism account)
references.context: The Baptism of Jesus narrative is the primary text for the entire sermon, detailing John the Baptist's baptism of Jesus and the voice from heaven declaring Jesus as beloved son.

references.name: Herod (the tyrant)
references.context: Historical figure referenced in the narrative of Jesus's infancy, the reason Joseph and Mary took Jesus to Egypt.

references.name: The Magi/Three Wise Men
references.context: Referenced as ancient astrologers from the east who charted a star and visited the infant Jesus on the Feast of the Epiphany.

references.name: John the Baptist
references.context: Identified as a prophet and baptizer in the River Jordan whose baptism was 'a rite or ritual of intensification and repentance and going in a new direction.'

references.name: River Jordan
references.context: The location where John baptized people as a symbolic act of turning around and receiving God's message.

references.name: The Voice from Heaven/Divine Voice
references.context: The heavenly proclamation 'you are my son, the beloved' that affirms Jesus's identity at his baptism.

references.name: The Dove
references.context: The symbol of the Holy Spirit descending upon Christ at baptism, described as 'sounding like the organ in the antiphonal organ, descends in this fluttering, beautiful way.'

references.name: World War II
references.context: Historical reference to the preacher's grandfather's military service as context for understanding a generation of men who did not express affection verbally.

references.name: Episcopal Church tradition
references.context: The theological and liturgical tradition cited regarding the practice of single baptism, never to be repeated, as a once-in-a-lifetime sacred identity-bestowing sacrament.

references.name: Catholic tradition
references.context: Referenced alongside Episcopal tradition regarding the practice of non-repetition of baptism as a sacred, identity-bestowing sacrament.

references.name: Sacramental theology
references.context: The implicit theological framework that baptism is 'the most sacred thing we do in church' in which 'God's power bestows an identity that is permanent and that will never ever change.'

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: Implicit in the statement 'because Christ's flesh is our flesh, it's an invitation to imagine that what Christ went through...was something remarkably similar to what we go through.'

references.name: Faith as epistemology
references.context: Stated explicitly: 'the Christian faith does not depend upon certainty' and the invitation to 'imagine by faith' what Christ experienced during the unknown years.

primary_theme: Baptism as a permanent divine identity-bestowing sacrament in which God unconditionally affirms each person as beloved, mirroring Christ's baptismal affirmation and inviting believers into the same experience of infinite, unconditional love.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with autobiographical narrative storytelling; the preacher uses personal family history (his non-affectionate grandfather and father, his emotionally expressive son) as a lens through which to interpret the theological meaning of baptism, creating an emotional bridge between the ancient gospel narrative and contemporary lived experience.

references.name: Alan Lightman
references.context: Physicist at MIT and novelist who wrote 'Einstein's Dreams'; used as an authority on the physics and fascination of light, particularly his childhood experiments measuring light speed and his observation that light travels between two breaths.

references.name: Einstein's Dreams
references.context: Novel by Alan Lightman that the preacher describes as 'wonderful' and expresses personal affection for.

references.name: Miller Williams
references.context: Poet cited for his short poem 'The Light in the Eyes' which the preacher describes as 'brilliant and playful' and uses to connect physical light to spiritual presence.

references.name: The Light in the Eyes
references.context: Short poem by Miller Williams consisting of two lines: 'The light in the eyes, / Who knows where it goes?' Used to explore the mystery of spiritual presence in human connection.

references.name: Gospel of John (Prologue)
references.context: John 1:1-5 (implied); the preacher quotes 'the true light, the light that enlightens everyone, was coming into the world' as a foundation for understanding Christ as incarnate light.

references.name: John 1:9
references.context: The phrase 'the light that enlightens everyone' is referenced repeatedly as the theological center of the sermon's argument about universal divine illumination.

references.name: Wedding at Cana
references.context: John 2:1-11; cited as the 'first sign' where Christ reveals hidden abundance (wine), illustrating how light takes shape as revelation and provision.

references.name: Healing of the blind man
references.context: John 9 (implied); referenced as an example of light taking 'the shape of healing' in the Gospel narrative.

references.name: Feeding of the 5,000
references.context: John 6:1-14 (implied); cited as example of light taking 'the shape of justice' through universal provision.

references.name: Martha, Mary, and Lazarus
references.context: John 11 (implied); mentioned as Jesus's friends, illustrating light taking the shape of friendship and grief (Lazarus's resurrection).

references.name: Death and Resurrection of Christ
references.context: John 19-20 (implied); referenced as the 'bookend to the birth' where transcendent light shines 'in the valley of the shadow of death' after three days.

references.name: Psalm 23:4
references.context: Implicit reference to 'valley of the shadow of death' in discussion of resurrection and light shining even in death.

references.name: Holy Communion
references.context: Referenced as one of the places where the light can be recognized and experienced within the Christian practice and liturgy.

references.name: Charles Connick
references.context: Famous stained glass artist who designed the cathedral's clear story windows; quoted as saying they are 'playgrounds for the afternoon sun.'

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: The central theological framework: abstract transcendent light becomes concrete and personal in the birth and person of Christ, taking specific shapes (healing, justice, friendship, resurrection).

references.name: Sacramental theology
references.context: Implicit throughout; the sermon treats material reality (stained glass, bread, wine, human eyes, light) as vehicles for divine presence and grace.

references.name: Christian mysticism
references.context: The emphasis on seeing light in the eyes of others, recognizing divine presence in ordinary human connection, and the transcendent made immanent reflects mystical theology.

primary_theme: Christ as incarnate light that progressively takes concrete shape throughout the Gospel of John—in healing, justice, friendship, and resurrection—and whose recognition in ourselves and others constitutes the true meaning of Christmas.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with poetic contemplation; the preacher uses narrative storytelling (Lightman's childhood experiment), literary analysis (Miller Williams's poem), biblical narrative arc analysis, and architectural observation (stained glass windows) to build a unified theological meditation on light as both physical phenomenon and spiritual reality.

references.name: Genesis 1
references.context: Cited as opening biblical reference: 'In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth, Genesis 1.'

references.name: Ecclesiastes 3
references.context: Preacher's favorite passage on time: 'For everything there is a time and a season, a time to be born and a time to die, a time to weep and a time to laugh, a time to keep silence and a time to speak.'

references.name: Matthew 28:20
references.context: Jesus's promise of presence: 'Lo, I am with you always, even unto the end of the ages.'

references.name: Matthew and Luke (Virgin Birth accounts)
references.context: Gospel accounts of the virgin birth: 'Whoever recounted the virgin birth in Matthew and in Luke, and it's only in Matthew and in Luke...'

references.name: Joseph (Old Testament)
references.context: Referenced as a dreamer whose dreams changed his life: 'Joseph in the Old Testament and the New Testament is a dreamer.'

references.name: Luke 1:26-38 (Angel Gabriel and Mary)
references.context: Referenced indirectly when discussing angels coming to Mary about Christ's conception: 'Another angel named Gabriel to Mary when Mary visited with Elizabeth about the conception of Christ.'

references.name: Matthew 1:20-24 (Angel to Joseph)
references.context: Angel comes to Joseph in a dream to explain the virgin birth and the name Emmanuel.

references.name: Matthew 2:13 (Angel warns Joseph to flee)
references.context: Referenced when discussing how the angel changes the direction of Joseph and Mary's life to a safer place after Jesus is born.

references.name: Isaiah 7:14 (Emmanuel prophecy)
references.context: The name 'Emmanuel, which means God is with us' - the hinge upon which the whole story turns.

references.name: Unnamed favorite theologian
references.context: Cited regarding the virgin birth: 'My favorite theologian said once that whoever recounted the virgin birth in Matthew and in Luke, the last thing that author had in mind was Parthenogenesis.'

references.name: Episcopal priest (unnamed)
references.context: Author of a book called 'Dreams, God's Forgotten Language' written 'a couple decades ago.'

references.name: Dreams: God's Forgotten Language
references.context: Book by an Episcopal priest asserting that dreams are 'God's forgotten language' - one of the most ancient and powerful ways the divine communicates to us in sleep.

references.name: His Dark Materials
references.context: Fantasy series by Philip Pullman: 'I love this series by Philip Pullman called His Dark Materials' - used to illustrate how angels come to us from the periphery and are best seen indirectly.

references.name: Philip Pullman
references.context: Author of His Dark Materials; noted as 'actually an agnostic' who 'nails this religiously and spiritually' the nature of how angels come to us.

references.name: Parthenogenesis
references.context: Scientific/biological term invoked to dismiss literalist interpretation of virgin birth: 'That is a fancy word for reproduction... This is not a story about what did or didn't happen in a tent or a bedroom.'

references.name: Mystical theology of dreams and intuition
references.context: Preacher wonders: 'If intuition, a gut feeling is a kind of message from an angel' - invokes mystical tradition of divine communication through subtle inner experience.

references.name: Architectural theology
references.context: Metaphorical use of cathedral architecture to understand one's place in God's larger fabric: 'When we walk in this cathedral, one is so aware of just the architecture and becoming a small but important part of this larger fabric.'

references.name: Hal Medling (former senior warden)
references.context: Pilot and church leader whose advice 'order a cup of coffee' when facing potential emergencies teaches not to overreact but to reflect and not react.

primary_theme: God is intimately involved in the passage of time and in our individual lives, revealed through the Advent story of the virgin birth and the name Emmanuel ('God is with us'), inviting us to recognize divine mystery in subtle, peripheral moments rather than obvious ones.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combining narrative storytelling (Hal Medling story, Philip Pullman reference), theological interpretation (virgin birth as mystery not magic), biblical architecture (how scripture maps time), and contemplative invitation (recognizing angels and dreams as divine communication in our own lives).

references.name: John the Baptist
references.context: On the Second Sunday of Advent, John the Baptist 'always shows up' as the liturgical figure; the preacher argues we cannot understand the historical John the Baptist unless we recognize and listen to contemporary prophets.

references.name: The Baptismal Covenant
references.context: The preacher cites the baptismal covenant as the foundational promise all Christians make: 'to strive for justice and peace among all people,' establishing that prophetic witness is not merely a prophet's vocation but a baptized Christian's responsibility.

references.name: Biblical prophecy / prophetic tradition
references.context: The sermon draws on the prophetic tradition in which prophets speak from the wilderness with a consistent message: 'you better turn around' (repentance/metanoia) and ask the central question 'Are your words and actions relieving suffering?'

references.name: Advent liturgy
references.context: The sermon is framed within the liturgical season of Advent and specifically 'the Second Sunday of Advent,' which traditionally features John the Baptist in the lectionary readings.

references.name: Repentance (metanoia)
references.context: The preacher emphasizes that 'repent' means 'turn around' and 'go in a different direction,' drawing on the Greek theological concept of metanoia as fundamental spiritual reorientation.

references.name: The concept of 'wilderness' in biblical/prophetic tradition
references.context: Prophets 'speak always from the wilderness' which the preacher describes as a metaphorical space that 'changes in terms of race, gender identity'—invoking the biblical tradition of wilderness as a place of spiritual testing and prophetic emergence.

primary_theme: In the season of Advent, contemporary prophets (like Charles Battle II's mother) call us to recognize our complicity in systemic injustice and to understand that all baptized Christians are called to prophetic witness that relieves suffering and pursues justice.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling with pastoral reflection and prophetic challenge; the preacher builds his theological argument through concrete contemporary testimony (the show-ups event and Charles Battle II's case) rather than abstract doctrine, culminating in a call to repentance and action grounded in liturgical tradition and baptismal vows.

references.name: The Serenity Prayer
references.context: Preacher recommends praying this prayer during the holidays, quoting it in full: 'God, grant me the serenity to accept the things I cannot change, the courage to change the things I can, and the wisdom to know the difference.'

references.name: Reinhold Niebuhr
references.context: Identified as 'a great theologian and ethicist in New York at Union Seminary' who wrote the Serenity Prayer.

references.name: Twelve-Step Recovery Movement (AA/NA)
references.context: Preacher notes the Serenity Prayer is 'a beloved prayer in the recovery movement, a beloved prayer in AA and NA.'

references.name: Book of Common Prayer
references.context: Preacher mentions wanting to write a new table grace for 'the next prayer book revision' to be included in the back of the Book of Common Prayer, combined with the Serenity Prayer.

references.name: Gospel of John
references.context: Referenced for the scene where Pilate asks 'What is truth?' and leaves the room before encountering the answer, illustrating the interplay between truth and power.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: The sermon's primary Gospel text for the day, featuring the conversation between Jesus and the two criminals on the cross, where one says 'Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom' and Jesus replies 'Truly I tell you, today you will be with me in paradise.'

references.name: Passion narratives (all four Gospels)
references.context: Preacher discusses 'the four gospel accounts of the cross, of the crucifixion of Jesus' and notes that 'the interplay between truth and power' is essential to understanding them.

references.name: The theology of the cross
references.context: Central theological framework: 'our Lord reigns from the cross that is his throne' and truth transcends power, particularly Roman imperial power.

references.name: Christian soteriology and eschatology
references.context: Implicit reference to redemption and resurrection theology through the image of God remembering us in paradise with 'healing and justice and love.'

references.name: Farmer's Almanac
references.context: Preacher mentions consulting the Farmer's Almanac to confirm that 'Thanksgiving is as late as it can possibly be this year.'

primary_theme: On Christ the King Sunday, the preacher argues that truth transcends power, that we must let go of our illusions of control, and that God's memory—not human effort or authority—ultimately grounds our redemption and healing.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combining personal narrative (family stories), liturgical theology, and scriptural exegesis to move the congregation from concrete emotional experience to abstract theological insight.

references.name: Thomas Cranmer
references.context: First editor and author of many prayers in the Book of Common Prayer; Archbishop of Canterbury whose prayer about 'read, mark, learn, and inwardly digest the Scriptures' exemplifies Protestant Reformation theology.

references.name: Book of Common Prayer (First)
references.context: Source of the Collect paired with the day's biblical lessons; represents Protestant Anglican tradition of liturgical prayer.

references.name: Protestant Reformation
references.context: Historical movement that shaped Cranmer's prayers and the 'more Protestant feel of Anglicanism.'

references.name: Desmond Tutu
references.context: Archbishop of Cape Town in the Anglican Church of South Africa; fought apartheid, chaired the Truth and Reconciliation Commission; exemplifies the integration of justice and joy through personal witness.

references.name: Truth and Reconciliation Commission
references.context: South African institution chaired by Tutu 'for the healing and the restoration of justice in South Africa'; represents Tutu's commitment to restorative justice.

references.name: Frederick Buechner
references.context: SAS novelist and Presbyterian minister; author whose distinction between happiness (man-made) and joy (God-given, sudden, unrepeatable) is central to the sermon's theological argument about joy.

references.name: Buechner's writings on joy
references.context: Devotional and theological writing distinguishing joy from happiness; joy comes unbidden from God and the soul as God's presence within us.

references.name: Psalm (Twenty-Third Sunday after Pentecost)
references.context: Associates joy with creation (rivers, mountains), singing, hymnody, and music; one of the four biblical lessons for the day.

references.name: Apocalyptic literature
references.context: One of the four biblical lessons for the day is 'an apocalyptic lesson from the prophet'; preacher defers discussion as Advent approaches.

references.name: Paul's epistles
references.context: Biblical lesson concerns idleness; preacher references 'Paul's congregations' and his concerns about idleness but defers deeper discussion.

references.name: Gospel account of temple destruction in Jerusalem
references.context: One of the four biblical lessons paired with the Collect; concerns Jesus's prophecy about the destruction of the Jerusalem temple.

references.name: Episcopal/Anglican liturgy
references.context: House blessing ritual; preacher notes that 'not everything we do is scripted' and reflects on the spontaneous elements of liturgical practice.

references.name: Holy water blessing
references.context: Liturgical practice of sprinkling holy water during house blessings; ritual element of sacramental Christianity.

references.name: Grace and effort (theological concept)
references.context: Music in the cathedral described as 'a remarkable alchemy...a strange combination of grace and effort,' invoking the theological tension between divine and human agency.

references.name: Apartheid
references.context: South African system of racial segregation 'similar to ours in the United States that kept blacks and whites separated'; Tutu's prophetic resistance to this injustice.

primary_theme: Joy is a divine gift that emerges unexpectedly from the depths of justice and spiritual struggle, exemplified in the life of Desmond Tutu, and represents the soul's encounter with God's presence within creation.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling with theological reflection; the preacher uses a extended personal anecdote about meeting Desmond Tutu to illustrate the integration of justice and joy, supplemented by scholarly authority (Buechner) and liturgical tradition (Cranmer, Book of Common Prayer), creating a pastoral invitation to recognize joy as a spiritual reality distinct from manufactured happiness.

references.name: Time Magazine cover, 'Is God Dead?' (1966)
references.context: Opening reference to the 1966 Time magazine cover featuring the question 'Is God Dead?' as a cultural marker of religion's crisis in modernity.

references.name: John Macquarrie
references.context: Episcopal priest and intellectual in New York City (1966) who was an existentialist and translated Heidegger; published a 600-page systematic theology in response to the Time magazine question.

references.name: Martin Heidegger
references.context: German philosopher whose work was translated by Macquarrie, representing a deep philosophical well from which Macquarrie drank.

references.name: Existentialism
references.context: Intellectual tradition represented by Macquarrie's theological approach and his engagement with Heidegger.

references.name: Macquarrie's Systematic Theology (1966)
references.context: A 600-page work arguing that God is being itself rather than a being among beings, and that all existence owes its reality to God who is being.

references.name: Being itself / Ground of Being
references.context: Macquarrie's conclusion that 'God is being itself' and 'the source of being, the source of truth, therefore of honesty. The source of truth. Beauty, the source of goodness, therefore of morality.'

references.name: Luke 18:9-14 (Parable of the Pharisee and the Tax Collector)
references.context: The biblical parable that frames the entire sermon, contrasting two different ways of praying and two different conceptions of God.

references.name: Gospel references to Pharisees and Jesus
references.context: General reference to how 'the Pharisees and Jesus do not get along well' despite being 'somewhat similar' as interpreters of the law.

references.name: Confession of Sin (liturgical practice)
references.context: Referenced as a moment when 'we're invited to confess our sins against God and our neighbor,' requiring self-awareness and honesty about one's own failings.

references.name: Hypocrisy
references.context: The Pharisee 'is a representation of hypocrisy' — outward religious observance masking inner cruelty and self-righteousness.

references.name: Humility (authentic prayer)
references.context: The tax collector 'is a representation of humility, authentic prayer that grounds us and brings us to our knees.'

primary_theme: The sermon contrasts two fundamentally different theological images of God — God as a projection of human preferences versus God as the transcendent, mysterious ground of all being — and argues that how we conceive of God determines whether our prayer becomes prideful hypocrisy or humble authenticity.
rhetorical_style: Scholarly pastoral reflection combining intellectual theology (Macquarrie and existentialism) with biblical exposition and liturgical practice, using narrative contrast (Pharisee vs. tax collector) to illuminate contemporary spiritual dangers for religious practitioners.

references.name: Paul the Apostle
references.context: Preacher notes wrestling intellectually with Paul's theology while affirming his epistolary wisdom about generational faith transmission in 2 Timothy.

references.name: 2 Timothy (Epistle to Timothy)
references.context: Paul writes to Timothy remembering his grandmother Lois's faith and mother Eunice's faith, establishing intergenerational transmission of faith.

references.name: Anglican theology / Episcopal theology
references.context: Preacher identifies 'high tolerance for ambiguity' as embodying Anglican/Episcopal theological sensibility regarding faith and reality.

references.name: The New Testament
references.context: Preacher identifies two paths to faith in the New Testament: familial transmission and cross-cultural/racial transmission through Christ's blood and faith kinship.

references.name: Seminary education
references.context: Preacher notes personal shift after seminary from abstract/cerebral faith to narrative and people-based faith.

references.name: Education for Ministry (EFM)
references.context: Program described as 'a small group that thinks and prays and really wrestles with intellectual and moral issues,' led by Mary.

references.name: The Eucharist / Common Cup / Altar Rail
references.context: Sacramental practice becomes the focal point for inclusive hospitality and healing in the mid-1980s AIDS story from Memphis parish.

references.name: Jack Charlton
references.context: Parish member described as becoming 'a kind of poet' when discussing faith, who articulated the phrase 'high tolerance for ambiguity' about Mary.

references.name: Dr. Sonny Golden
references.context: Young physician who preached from the pulpit in the mid-1980s addressing AIDS transmission fears and affirmed continued sacramental communion.

references.name: Diane Cast
references.context: Altar guild chair of 25 years whose storytelling about deceased parishioners connects spiritual work to narrative memory and sacramental care.

references.name: Mary (parishioner)
references.context: EFM mentor described as having 'incredible mind' and combining intellectual rigor with heartfelt spirituality; exemplifies tolerating ambiguity in faith.

references.name: Scales falling from eyes
references.context: Preacher uses this biblical phrase when Jack Charlton's insight about 'high tolerance for ambiguity' creates spiritual understanding.

references.name: Keep the feast
references.context: Dr. Golden's call to 'keep the feast' invokes eucharistic theology of inclusion and community binding.

primary_theme: Faith is primarily transmitted through relationships, stories, and lived exemplars rather than abstract theology, and the capacity to hold ambiguity with intellectual and spiritual maturity is essential to authentic Episcopal faith.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative storytelling combined with personal testimony and community exemplars; the preacher builds theological argument through accumulated anecdotes and character portraits rather than scholastic argument, creating intimacy and identifying local saints.

references.name: Amy Jill Levine
references.context: Scholar of New Testament at Vanderbilt Divinity School, expert on Jesus's parables; her conclusion that 'This parable defies a fully satisfactory explanation' frames the sermon's approach.

references.name: Amy Jill Levine's 'Short Stories'
references.context: Book about Jesus's parables; its title establishes the foundational claim that parables are 'short stories' not allegories to be reduced to moral lessons.

references.name: Stephen Dunn
references.context: Poet and college professor cited for his teaching that poems begin 'in the moment that you have surprised or startled yourself'—applied to the manager's moment of self-awareness.

references.name: Brené Brown
references.context: Scholar (Episcopalian) known for work on 'the profound distinction between guilt and shame'; cited to define shame as 'believing we are flawed and therefore unworthy of love and belonging.'

references.name: Brené Brown's work on guilt and shame
references.context: Brown's distinction that guilt is helpful (allows strategy change) while shame is debilitating (makes one unworthy of connection), directly applied to the manager's emotional state.

references.name: Mark's Gospel
references.context: Referenced for the statement that 'the way Jesus taught was in and through these short stories, these parables.'

references.name: Luke 15
references.context: Referenced as containing parables about finding lost things (coin, child) that bring 'incredible joy,' illustrating Jesus's attention to human emotional complexity.

references.name: Luke 16 (The Dishonest Manager parable)
references.context: The primary text being preached; includes the debtor manager's self-aware statement 'I'm not strong enough to dig, and I'm ashamed to beg' and the concluding moral about making 'friends for yourselves by means of dishonest wealth.'

references.name: Worried Man Blues
references.context: Old blues standard invoked as a musical characterization of the manager's emotional state ('This man is worried').

references.name: Literary/narrative criticism tradition
references.context: The sermon employs close narrative reading of the parable as a short story with character development, emotional arcs, and psychological realism rather than allegory.

references.name: Allegorical interpretation
references.context: Criticized as reducing stories to quick moral lessons ('do this, don't do that') that 'gut the allegory of any meaning and color and narrative'—contrasted with parable as short story.

primary_theme: Parables, especially the Dishonest Manager, are best understood as short stories that reveal Jesus's intimate knowledge of human psychology, emotion, and moral struggle rather than as allegories with extractable moral lessons.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative and literary analysis blended with scholarly reference; the preacher moves from textual exegesis through character psychology and contemporary scholarly voices (Levine, Brown) to theological reflection on what the parable reveals about Jesus as a storyteller who knows and loves humanity in its complexity.

references.name: Book of Exodus
references.context: Moses narrative where God reveals the divine name 'I will be who I will be' at the beginning of Exodus, foundational to understanding monotheism and mystery.

references.name: Exodus 33:18-23 (Moses seeing God's glory/back parts)
references.context: Preacher quotes the King James Version phrase 'I will pass by and I will show you my back parts' but my face shall never be seen' as foundational story about divine mystery.

references.name: Gospel of John (entire)
references.context: Described as 'almost like a riff upon that foundational story from Exodus about Moses' and the basis for understanding that God is known through the human and the ordinary.

references.name: John 11:1-44 (Lazarus resurrection narrative)
references.context: Story of Jesus with friends Mary, Martha, and Lazarus, illustrating friendship and intimacy as central to John's gospel.

references.name: John 11:35 ('Jesus wept')
references.context: Called 'the shortest verse in all of the Bible' and used to exemplify Christ's humanity, pathos, and intimacy in grief.

references.name: John 21 (post-resurrection dialogue with Peter and the beloved disciple)
references.context: Peter notices 'the disciple whom you loved' following them, the one who reclined at the Last Supper; preacher discusses the beloved disciple as anonymous for a reason.

references.name: Last Supper / Eucharist narrative
references.context: Referenced through the image of the beloved disciple reclining close to Jesus at table, portraying intimacy and physical closeness.

references.name: Hebrew Scriptures (general)
references.context: Moses journey with God and the revelation of God's name invoked as foundational to Jewish and Christian monotheism.

references.name: King James Version
references.context: Preacher notes this translation as unforgettable for its rendering of God's promise to Moses, though originally in Hebrew.

references.name: Christian sacramental theology (Baptism and Communion)
references.context: Preacher argues that sacraments play upon the theme that God is known through the ordinary: baptism as rebirth, communion as hungering for bread and companionship.

references.name: Mystical theology / apophatic theology
references.context: The entire sermon reflects the apophatic tradition: God is always cloaked in mystery, we never know all of who God is, only eternity might bring full intimacy with God.

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: Central theme that God is known 'in and through the human,' 'in and through the real,' and through Christ's ordinary life and flesh.

references.name: Barbara Harris
references.context: First female bishop in the Anglican Communion, consecrated in the 1980s in a controversial decision; preacher describes her as 89 years old, spiritually powerful, irreverent, colorful, and funny.

references.name: Kim Lucas (new bishop)
references.context: Preacher attended her ordination/consecration service a few months prior; mentioned as occasion for reflection on women's leadership in the church.

references.name: Anglican Communion
references.context: Referenced in context of Barbara Harris's consecration as controversial within Anglican and Roman Catholic traditions.

references.name: Roman Catholic Church
references.context: Mentioned as having disagreed with Barbara Harris's consecration as a female bishop.

references.name: Christian iconography / artistic portrayal
references.context: Preacher notes how artists and iconographers have portrayed Jesus with a disciple physically laid on his breast at the Lord's Supper.

primary_theme: God's invisible presence becomes known and intimate through the human, the ordinary, and Christ's flesh, yet always remains partly mysterious and unknowable.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling combined with pastoral reflection; preacher opens with an anecdote about a senior warden's humorous challenge, then moves to exegesis of foundational biblical texts (Exodus, John), and concludes with witness to contemporary examples (Barbara Harris, Kim Lucas), inviting congregants to see themselves as participants in divine intimacy.

references.name: Lectionary
references.context: The preacher notes that whoever 'planned or devised the lectionary' chose the theme of keeping lamps trimmed and burning as relevant for 'every season of our lives at all times and in all places.'

references.name: Paul's Letter to the Corinthians
references.context: The preacher cites Paul admonishing the Corinthians to 'maintain the status quo' because 'the end is near,' with specific commands about marriage, remaining unwed, and slavery.

references.name: 1 Corinthians (implied passage on end times)
references.context: Referenced as Paul's specific commands: 'If you're married, stay married. If you're unwed, stay unwed... if you're a slave, don't worry about freedom.'

references.name: Gospel reading (parable of the Master's return)
references.context: The text for the 'Ninth Sunday after Pentecost' that pairs the return of the master with the return of the heart.

references.name: Matthew 6:21 (or Luke 12:34)
references.context: The preacher quotes directly: 'For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also,' as the summary of the gospel reading.

references.name: Advent liturgical season
references.context: The preacher notes the gospel reading echoes Advent themes and the practice of keeping lamps trimmed and burning during the dark season before Christmas.

references.name: Christian sacraments (Baptism and Holy Communion)
references.context: Used as examples of how we 'meet Christ' in expected contexts, by analogy with meeting Christ in unexpected places.

references.name: Teilhard de Chardin
references.context: Identified as 'a paleontologist, of all things, and a Roman Catholic priest' whose advice is quoted: 'Trust in the slow work of God.'

references.name: Teilhard de Chardin, 'Trust in the slow work of God' (Prayer for Patience)
references.context: The preacher quotes the full passage: 'Trust in the slow work of God, and accept the anxiety of feeling yourself in suspense and incomplete,' and notes it was included in the sermon inserts.

references.name: Geological deep time (65 million year timeline of mountains)
references.context: The preacher uses the 88-year-old's fascination with the age of Colorado mountains (65 million years) to illustrate how 'time humbles us... making us feel small in a good way.'

references.name: Mystical theology of divine presence in unknowing
references.context: The idea that 'the unknown and the uncontrollable can become a means of grace, even a door to the divine' reflects apophatic or negative theology.

references.name: Conflict Resolution studies / Conflict Resolution Center at University of Denver
references.context: Referenced through Tamara Destray's work in the international studies program; her insight that 'every person in one way or another is armed' in the face of 'fundamental insecurity' models intellectual engagement with contemporary social problems.

primary_theme: In the face of uncertainty and the delayed return of Christ, Christians should trust in God's slow work, keep their hearts focused on spiritual treasures rather than worldly security, and recognize that faith is opposed to certainty rather than doubt.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with narrative storytelling (anecdotes about the 88-year-old hiker and conversation with Tamara Destray) and prophetic challenge (gun control advocacy), grounded in scriptural and theological authority.

references.name: Book of Amos
references.context: The preacher uses Amos 8:11-13 as the primary biblical text, where Amos receives a vision of summer fruit that symbolizes God's judgment on Israel for economic injustice toward the poor.

references.name: Amos 8:11-13 (The Famine Vision)
references.context: God prophesies through Amos: 'The songs of the temple shall be turned into wailings' and there will be 'a famine on the land...not a famine of bread...but of hearing the words of the Lord.'

references.name: Christian Community Practice of Boundary-Setting
references.context: The preacher reflects that 'every Christian community, just like any group, has to have boundaries' and that 'boundaries at their best actually create space on both sides of the line.'

references.name: Sacred Space as Locus for Emotional Expression
references.context: The preacher suggests that churches are 'a community where we come to offer the deepest emotions we have' and that this is 'a time-honored practice' in sacred places.

references.name: Prophetic Tradition (Ancient Israel)
references.context: The preacher references the prophetic tradition generally when noting 'Amos, as prophets often do, sees a vision,' evoking the broader Old Testament prophetic literature.

references.name: Economic Justice / Compassion Ethics
references.context: The sermon's critique mirrors liberation theology and prophetic theology when the preacher emphasizes God's condemnation: 'You've made no space within your community...for the poor...Your economy matters more than compassion and people.'

references.name: Empathy and Neighbor-Love Theology
references.context: The preacher invokes the principle that we should 'recognize our neighbors as ourselves and ourselves as our neighbors,' echoing Leviticus 19:18 and the Great Commandment tradition.

primary_theme: Sacred communities must create space for the deepest human emotions—especially those of the poor and marginalized—or risk spiritual famine; true Christian practice recognizes shared humanity with outsiders and prioritizes compassion over institutional boundaries.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative storytelling grounded in prophetic biblical interpretation; the preacher opens with a concrete personal anecdote (the disruption during morning service), uses it as a hermeneutical lens to re-read the Book of Amos, and moves toward existential and ethical reflection on empathy, homelessness, and community responsibility.

references.name: Mark Twain
references.context: Quoted for the witty observation that 'in the beginning God made man in his own image, and man, being a gentleman ever since then, has been returning the favor,' illustrating how humans project their own qualities onto God.

references.name: Carl Jung
references.context: Referenced for the concept of 'the unconscious' as 'that underground river of memories and images' that constitutes a cosmos within ourselves as mysterious as the heavens.

references.name: Mary Oliver
references.context: Identified as a favorite poet whose poem about angels is presented as fitting for Trinity Sunday; her work alludes to Episcopal language and ritualism.

references.name: Mary Oliver, 'Angels' poem (untitled or from a collection)
references.context: A poem about angels included in the sermon insert, quoted extensively as the sermon's closing meditation on how Trinity language functions imaginatively rather than as doctrinal proof.

references.name: John's Gospel (end passage, John 21:25)
references.context: The verse stating that Jesus 'did a lot of other things' such that 'the world itself could not contain the amount of books that would have to be written,' used to illustrate how Christ's story is inseparable from human stories.

references.name: The Gospels (general)
references.context: Referenced as Sunday readings that open a lens onto human stories and reveal the depth of unconditional love in Christ's life, death, and resurrection.

references.name: Genesis 1:27 (implicit)
references.context: Alluded to in Mark Twain's paraphrase about God making humans 'in his own image,' foundational to the sermon's discussion of anthropomorphic language for God.

references.name: Christian liturgy and hymnody
references.context: Critiqued as having become so fixed on masculine and paternal imagery for God that the wider range of biblical and theological feminine images (such as God as mother) have been neglected.

references.name: Christian mysticism
references.context: Invoked through reference to 'saints and mystics' who teach that 'God is closer to us than we are to ourselves,' particularly in the context of Spirit language.

references.name: Apophatic theology / negative theology (implicit)
references.context: Underlying the sermon's insistence that Trinity language should not be literalized but understood as metaphorical 'chapter titles' pointing beyond themselves to mystery.

references.name: The Lectionary
references.context: Referenced as the church's appointed biblical readings, noted as rarely including the final verse of John's Gospel.

references.name: Episcopal theology and practice
references.context: The sermon is situated within an Episcopal context (preached at an Episcopal cathedral named after John's Gospel) and reflects on Episcopal liturgical language and imagery.

primary_theme: The Trinity (Father, Son, Holy Spirit) should be understood as imaginative metaphorical 'chapter titles' rather than literal doctrinal definitions, each pointing to a dimension of human experience with God: Creator (source of being), Christ (unconditional love), and Spirit (immanent presence), while acknowledging the limitations and dangers of masculine-gendered language for divinity.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with narrative storytelling (the Berthoud Pass anecdote, personal confession about experiencing God as abstract), woven together with theological critique (problems with paternal God-language), imaginative reframing (chapter-title metaphor), and lyrical conclusion (Mary Oliver poem). The preacher balances intellectual rigor with accessibility, moving from logical argument to embodied experience to poetic invocation.

references.name: Prophet Joel
references.context: Peter explains the Pentecost experience by invoking Joel's prophecy: 'The Spirit would descend upon all flesh. Your children will have visions. Your old men will dream dreams.'

references.name: Joel 2:28-29
references.context: Direct quotation of Joel's prophecy about the Spirit being poured out on all flesh, sons and daughters having visions, slaves receiving the Spirit and prophesying.

references.name: Acts 2 (Pentecost narrative)
references.context: The primary biblical text undergirding the entire sermon, detailing the descent of the Holy Spirit on the apostles and the multilingual audience from Mesopotamia, Cappadocia, Phrygia, and Pamphylia.

references.name: Peter
references.context: Cited as the speaker who explains that the Pentecost phenomenon is not drunkenness but the fulfillment of Joel's prophecy, with the famous line 'It's nine in the morning' (or 'eight in the morning').

references.name: Mary Magdalene
references.context: Referenced as a biblical figure who 'was at the empty tomb,' used to illustrate how spiritual gifts are not hierarchical.

references.name: Saint Peter
references.context: Mentioned as a called saint whose spiritual status is not superior to that of ordinary believers, illustrating the non-hierarchical nature of the Spirit.

references.name: Pseudo-Dionysius (5th or 6th century philosopher)
references.context: The preacher references Pseudo-Dionysius's book 'The Divine Names' and his teaching that 'the soul moves in a circle' because it constantly incorporates external experience into itself.

references.name: The Divine Names
references.context: Pseudo-Dionysius's work about why God is called what God is called, specifically cited for its discussion of the Holy Spirit and the soul's circular movement.

references.name: Paul (Apostle)
references.context: Quoted at the sermon's conclusion: 'It's always the Spirit of God interacting with our very spirits,' describing the internalization of divine experience.

references.name: Paul's teachings on the Spirit
references.context: Invoked in the closing statement about the Spirit of God interacting with human spirits, likely referencing Romans 8:16 or similar passages.

references.name: Mystical/Contemplative tradition
references.context: The emphasis on prayer, contemplation, reflection, and the internalization of experience as pathways to spiritual knowledge reflects mystical spirituality.

references.name: Neo-Platonic philosophy
references.context: Pseudo-Dionysius's circular soul metaphor and the concept of the soul moving inward to incorporate divine experience reflects Neo-Platonic thought.

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: The sermon's emphasis on the Spirit dwelling within each person and the need to internalize divine mystery reflects incarnational theological anthropology.

primary_theme: The Holy Spirit operates as a translator and unifier that enables people across all differences—language, social status, geography, and religion—to recognize and understand one another by translating external experience into internal spiritual knowledge that dwells within each person.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection grounded in narrative storytelling (anecdotes about seminary friends, conversations with other priests, observations about music and translation) interwoven with scholarly references (Pseudo-Dionysius) and contemplative theology, creating an accessible yet intellectually substantive meditation on Pentecost.

references.name: names.org
references.context: Opening reference to a website listing popular baby names for 2019, used to establish that 'Judas' remains unpopular due to historical association with betrayal.

references.name: Four Gospels (Matthew, Mark, Luke, John)
references.context: Preacher references 'all four Gospels' in relation to Judas Iscariot's betrayal of Jesus with a kiss.

references.name: Saint Jude tradition
references.context: Judas (not Iscariot) becomes Saint Jude, patron saint for lost causes, a 'wonderful, really lovely tradition' that emerged in later Christian tradition.

references.name: John's Gospel
references.context: The sermon is based on a Gospel reading from John and extensively explores John's theological wrestling with crisis of identity in early churches.

references.name: Gospel of John (authorship/dating question)
references.context: Preacher notes this gospel was 'probably written the latest compared to the other three' and addresses why John wrestles with the end of resurrection appearances and death of apostles.

references.name: Peter's martyrdom allusion (John 21:18-19)
references.context: Referenced via 'I tell you, Peter, one day you will not go where you want to go. Others will lead you'—John's gospel alludes to Peter's eventual death.

references.name: Jewish scriptures
references.context: Preacher notes the Holy Spirit as 'an invisible animating presence that's been with us from the very history...throughout the Jewish scriptures.'

references.name: Anne Ulanov
references.context: Described as 'a depth psychologist who's a theologian' and Episcopalian whose work on prayer the preacher 'loves a lot'; one of the new bishop's favorite professors.

references.name: Primary Speech
references.context: Ulanov's book on 'praying in these authentic ways'; the preacher cites her thesis that 'prayer opens a door to the unconscious images that are in us.'

references.name: Depth psychology / Jungian tradition
references.context: Implicit framework through Anne Ulanov's work on unconscious images, dreams, and the psyche in relation to prayer and spirituality.

references.name: Jazz musician/theologian (unnamed)
references.context: A jazz musician serving as consultant for the cathedral's music program who offered the extemporaneous prayer: 'Descend, O Spirit, into our lived experience.'

references.name: The Wilderness (evening service)
references.context: Reference to a dynamic evening service at the cathedral that expanded its musical repertoire to include jazz.

references.name: Incarnational theology / Christology
references.context: The sermon's core argument that Christ, through the Holy Spirit, is 'at home in us' deep in our souls and bodies.

references.name: Pneumatology (doctrine of the Holy Spirit)
references.context: Central theological framework: John's response to the crisis of the apostles' deaths is the Holy Spirit as guide, guardian, and enlightener.

references.name: Liturgical calendar / Easter season
references.context: Reference to how 'the Easter season ends for us' and the church 'move[s] on to other themes' in the liturgical calendar, mirroring the end of resurrection appearances.

references.name: Matthew 22:37-39 (Love of neighbor and self)
references.context: Implicit reference: 'How can we love our neighbor if we don't love ourselves?' echoes the greatest commandment tradition.

references.name: Exodus / breath imagery
references.context: Implicit reference to ruach/pneuma (breath/spirit) in biblical tradition; preacher notes 'that's what spirit primarily means. It's the breath that is within us.'

references.name: Second-generation leadership crisis
references.context: Preacher notes 'What happens when we move to second generation leadership?' as a perennial crisis in 'families and communities and even businesses.'

references.name: Contemplative prayer tradition
references.context: Prayer described not as 'spiritual housekeeping' but as confrontation with 'all that's within us that's ambiguous and dark and confusing and mysterious.'

references.name: Self-awareness / introspection
references.context: Preacher emphasizes 'Growing in self-awareness is not navel-gazing' but 'one of the greatest gifts that you can give to your neighbor.'

primary_theme: The Holy Spirit dwelling within human souls and bodies as the continuing presence of Christ after the apostolic age, accessible through prayer and self-awareness.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with scholarly exegesis, using narrative anecdotes (baby names, jazz musician's prayer), theological tradition, and invitations to contemplative practice to make abstract doctrine experientially intimate.

references.name: Notre Dame Cathedral
references.context: Opening image of Holy Week: the burning of Notre Dame, a 'beautiful and marvelous cathedral named after the mother of our Lord,' used to establish the week's confusion and disorientation.

references.name: The Bible as a book of questions
references.context: Preacher cites 'a novelist that I enjoy' who observes 'the Bible, more than anything, is a book of questions, more so than a book of answers,' framing his theological method.

references.name: Genesis 3:9 (God's question to Adam and Eve)
references.context: 'Where are you?' / 'Where art thou?' — God's question to Adam and Eve east of Eden, identified as the echoing question of the week and turned back upon God in suffering.

references.name: John's Gospel
references.context: Sermon takes place on Easter Sunday at St. John's Cathedral, hearing 'John's story of the resurrection,' with John specifically identified as 'the only one of the four gospel writers' who emphasizes darkness at the tomb.

references.name: John 20 (Resurrection narrative)
references.context: Detailed exegesis of Mary Magdalene going to the tomb 'while it was still dark,' the darkness as 'an essential piece' of John's account, and the moment of recognition by her name.

references.name: Genesis 2 (The Garden)
references.context: Preacher notes the tomb surrounded by a garden, connecting to the beginning of time when 'God is up to something remarkable or interesting, there was a garden,' establishing intertextual resonance.

references.name: Mary Magdalene as Jesus' student
references.context: Characterized as 'one of Jesus' students...certainly one of his brightest,' a woman of 'remarkable intellect' and 'remarkable courage,' departing from traditional interpretations.

references.name: Rational, analytical method / Episcopalian tradition
references.context: Preacher self-identifies as 'a very rational Episcopalian' who 'tends to overthink things,' later deciding 'the best way to come at it is indirectly, and not overthink it.'

references.name: Mystery vs. Magic
references.context: Preacher distinguishes the resurrection as 'mystery, not magic,' and notes 'a part of the mystery is the way that this story actually relates to our own stories.'

references.name: Incarnational/Sacramental theology
references.context: The preacher's emphasis on a single word ('Miriam') spoken with 'unimaginable tenderness' as the hinge of the entire resurrection narrative suggests a theology of presence and recognition rather than proof.

references.name: Columbine High School shooting
references.context: Referenced as historical/contextual trauma ('Columbine on her mind') affecting Denver schools and families, establishing the concrete suffering undergirding theological questioning.

primary_theme: In the midst of suffering and unanswerable questions, the resurrection is encountered not through rational proof but through recognition and faith—a story told in darkness that invites us to find ourselves within it.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling combined with pastoral reflection, moving from contemporary trauma to extended personal anecdote (cliff-jumping) as indirect theological analogy; employs intimate, conversational tone while weaving together textual analysis, self-disclosure, and existential questioning.

references.name: Council of Nicaea (325 CE)
references.context: The preacher cites the Council of Nicaea's establishment of a uniform Easter date based on the vernal equinox and first spring full moon, establishing the historical foundation for the night's observance.

references.name: Eucharistic Prayer
references.context: The preacher references the liturgical phrase 'in the fullness of time' used during the Eucharistic prayer to illustrate God's revelation of new perspective through Jesus.

references.name: Canon Broderick
references.context: The preacher cites Canon Broderick's Good Friday sermon claim that the cross represents God's empathetic participation in human suffering: 'I relate, I really feel what you feel, and I feel it from the inside.'

references.name: Alcoholics Anonymous (AA)
references.context: The preacher invokes AA as a contemporary model of spiritual recovery, using a parishioner's testimony about how hearing others' stories and recognizing resurrection in this life enabled her recovery.

references.name: Christian liturgical tradition / Easter Vigil
references.context: The entire sermon is framed around 'The Great Vigil of Easter,' drawing on ancient Christian practices of night vigil and sacramental remembrance.

references.name: Christian eschatology
references.context: The preacher develops the theological concept that eternity frames chronological time, and that resurrection operates within both temporal and eternal dimensions.

references.name: Sacramental theology
references.context: The preacher references resurrection as 'a sign and sacrament and symbol of what God is doing everywhere... throughout the entire cosmos.'

primary_theme: Easter's resurrection promise extends beyond eschatological hope to reveal God's redemptive work within present time, inviting believers to take the long view of eternity and recognize resurrection as an ongoing spiritual reality accessible within this life.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with narrative storytelling; the preacher uses historical scholarship (Council of Nicaea), liturgical reference, and personal anecdote (the woman in recovery) to build a theological argument about time, resurrection, and spiritual transformation.

references.name: Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone / The Sorcerer's Stone
references.context: Used as opening illustration; preacher references the Mirror of Erised from Book One, which shows people their deepest desires rather than reflections.

references.name: Mirror of Erised
references.context: Extended metaphor throughout sermon; 'Erised' spelled backwards is 'desire'—used to frame the theme of desiring what God promises.

references.name: Dumbledore
references.context: Character from Harry Potter cited as describing the mirror's magic: showing 'the deepest desires of our hearts.'

references.name: The Collect of the Fifth Sunday in Lent
references.context: Described as 'one of the most beautiful and one of the oldest,' calling for ability to love what God commands and desire what God promises.

references.name: Psalm 126
references.context: Paired with the Collect; opens with 'When the Lord restored the fortunes of Zion, then were we like those who dream' and includes references to laughter and joy.

references.name: Joseph (Old Testament)
references.context: Referenced as 'a dreamer and an interpreter of dreams' in Holy Scripture.

references.name: Joseph (New Testament/Jesus' stepfather)
references.context: Mentioned as warned in a dream to avoid Herod and go to Egypt.

references.name: Wallace and Gene Clift
references.context: Two priests who worked at the cathedral and did extensive work on dreams and the unconscious; preacher learned about them through their books.

references.name: Symbols of Transformation in Dreams
references.context: Work by Wallace and Gene Clift; emphasized that dreams represent dialogue with the unconscious, not just practical direction.

references.name: Dreams as dialogue with the unconscious
references.context: Clift's theological anthropology; dreams described as encounters with 'that river that's just below the level of our ego and mind' where 'we encounter the divine.'

references.name: John 11 (Lazarus narrative)
references.context: Extended story of Jesus weeping at Lazarus's death and raising him to life; used as parable/image of what resurrection might entail.

references.name: Jesus wept (John 11:35)
references.context: Cited as 'the shortest verse in the Bible' and 'easiest one to memorize'; represents Jesus's deep friendship and human emotion.

references.name: Paul (Apostle)
references.context: Contrasted with John's Gospel as working 'by way of ideas' rather than symbol and story; described as sometimes 'a little bit boring' for some.

references.name: Philippians 3 (implied)
references.context: Paraphrased: Paul desires to know the power of Christ and Christ's resurrection, to become like Christ in death, seeking the resurrection of the dead.

references.name: John's Gospel (John 11)
references.context: Contrasted with Paul as working through symbol and story; centers on resurrection mystery through narrative of Lazarus.

references.name: The Reverend Dr. Keith Ward
references.context: Regis Professor of Divinity in Church of England; published author who wrestles with central mysteries of Christian faith in conversation with other religions.

references.name: Religion and Community
references.context: Work by Keith Ward describing resurrection as 'a form of existence beyond space and time' while being 'something entirely new and unimaginable.'

references.name: Rational theology / reinterpretation of Christian mysteries
references.context: Ward's approach to make central Christian mysteries 'rational and intelligible to people in the 21st century.'

references.name: Holy Communion / Eucharist
references.context: Referenced as a 'mystery' that suggests resurrection will include embodiment, sociality, memory, and complex consciousness.

references.name: Bishop Rob O'Neill
references.context: Mentioned in passing as having buried Wallace Clift, with preacher's assistance.

primary_theme: On the Fifth Sunday in Lent, we are invited to examine and cultivate our deepest desires—specifically, to desire God's promises of resurrection, which includes embodied friendship, community, memory, and the assurance that we are eternally held in God's love.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling combined with scholarly reflection and pastoral theology; the preacher opens with a popular cultural reference (Harry Potter) to make abstract theological concepts accessible, then moves through layers of theological tradition (liturgical collect, psalm, Pauline epistle, Johannine narrative) while weaving in conversations with contemporary theologians and personal anecdotes about dreams and laughter.

references.name: Episcopal Prayer Book
references.context: The preacher notes that the Prayer Book emphasizes Lent as penitence and preparation, particularly for baptism and confirmation.

references.name: Alexandrian liturgical tradition
references.context: A liturgical historian discovered that in the Alexandrian tradition, Lent was a 40-day post-Epiphany fast mirroring Jesus' wilderness fast, making it a 'looking back season' rather than a preparation season.

references.name: Jesus' 40-day post-baptismal fast in the wilderness
references.context: Referenced as the model for the Alexandrian Lenten tradition; Jesus faced temptation from the devil while guided by the Holy Spirit.

references.name: Epiphany
references.context: Described as the season looking back to Jesus' baptism, God's incarnation in a baby, the star that led the Magi, and divine light drawing the Gentiles into incarnation.

references.name: The Magi and the Star
references.context: Referenced as part of Epiphany tradition showing divine light in the heavens drawing the Magi and Gentiles into the incarnation.

references.name: Ash Wednesday
references.context: Mentioned as an intensely rich and dramatic liturgical moment involving ashing, which the preacher suggests requires an indirect approach.

references.name: Emotional intelligence
references.context: The preacher references psychologists' term for emotional intelligence when describing his grandmother's capacity to feel and choose emotional responses appropriately.

references.name: Secularism
references.context: The preacher discusses contemporary discourse about secularism, redefining it not as inability to believe but as inability to feel.

references.name: Resurrection and the human body
references.context: The preacher affirms that resurrection is always located in 'the human body and soul,' connecting Lenten remembrance to Easter preparation.

primary_theme: Lent as a season of remembering and feeling one's past experiences through the body, rather than primarily preparing for the future, drawing on the Alexandrian liturgical tradition that emphasizes looking back to Epiphany and Jesus' baptism.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection combining personal anecdote (grandmother, parishioner in crisis, new sacristan), liturgical scholarship, and contemplative theology to invite emotional and embodied spiritual practice.

references.name: Philo of Alexandria
references.context: Preacher quotes Philo: 'Be kind, for everyone you meet is fighting a great battle,' reflecting on a funeral service encounter.

references.name: Marion Hatchett
references.context: Liturgist who trained the preacher; taught at Sewanee; said 'no one leaves church humming the sermon' to encourage creative liturgical practice.

references.name: Michael Curry
references.context: Presiding Bishop of The Episcopal Church, described as 'of royal wedding fame,' will serve as chief consecrator for Bishop Kim Lucas's ordination.

references.name: Kim Lucas
references.context: Bishop-elect whose ordination and consecration will be hosted at St. John's Cathedral on May 18, 2019.

references.name: Silent Night
references.context: Hymn referenced as example of familiar, emotionally powerful shared singing on Christmas Eve.

references.name: Deep River
references.context: African American spiritual chosen as anthem for Martin Luther King Jr. weekend Evensong, arranged by an Episcopal organist, used to hold communal grief.

references.name: The Bible
references.context: Referenced implicitly regarding the need to 'read all of it' and to 'sing all of it,' including unfamiliar passages.

references.name: Christ / Jesus
references.context: Referenced throughout: Christ's call for 'all that we are and all that we have,' spiritual connection through Eucharistic giving.

references.name: The Eucharist
references.context: Referenced as the central liturgical act where giving money is framed as a holy privilege and connection to Christ.

references.name: All Souls Day / All Souls practice
references.context: Referenced as a liturgical connection point for parishioners, particularly those with columbarium spots or loose affiliations.

references.name: Episcopal Church liturgy and practice
references.context: Implicit throughout: ordination, consecration, Evensong, committal services, vestry structures, and liturgical roles (dean's verger, senior warden).

references.name: Liturgical theology
references.context: Underlying framework: the idea that liturgy and shared worship shape human emotions and create communal bonds; echoed in Marion Hatchett's teaching.

primary_theme: The cathedral's dual role as both diocesan institution and parish community is fulfilled through liturgical practices—particularly singing and ritual—that allow people to share profound emotions (grief and joy) and connect spiritually with one another and with Christ.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with narrative storytelling; the preacher uses personal anecdotes (the funeral committal service, Marion Hatchett's mentorship, Christmas Eve singing) to illustrate theological points about emotional communion and liturgical participation, moving between administrative reporting and spiritual exhortation.

references.name: John the Baptist
references.context: Referenced as the baptizer whose baptism was about repentance and forgiveness of sins, establishing the logical puzzle of why Jesus would need baptism.

references.name: Jesus Christ
references.context: Central figure whose baptism in the River Jordan is the subject of the sermon; portrayed as entering into human existence and suffering.

references.name: Mary and Joseph
references.context: Mentioned as witnesses to Jesus' birth, illustrating his entrance into human experience.

references.name: The Wise Men
references.context: Mentioned as witnesses to Jesus' birth alongside Mary and Joseph.

references.name: Leviathan
references.context: Biblical sea monster referenced as a symbol of the deepest dangers and darkest waters that Jesus enters with us; alluded to as dwelling in the deep Tennessee River.

references.name: River Jordan
references.context: The specific location where Jesus was baptized according to the opening collect; contrasted with the Tennessee River as narrow and shallow rather than wide and deep.

references.name: Opening Collect of the Day
references.context: Liturgical prayer cited as the source for the baptism narrative, establishing Jesus' baptism in the River Jordan.

references.name: Follow thou me
references.context: Biblical call of discipleship cited as the traditional emphasis of Christian preaching and the great call to follow Jesus.

references.name: Do not be afraid. I am with you always.
references.context: Comfort words attributed to Christ, offering reassurance in solitude and darkness; likely Matthew 28:20 or similar commission passage.

references.name: Advent sermons
references.context: Referenced as typical context for preaching about repentance and turning around in the liturgical calendar.

references.name: Methodist church tradition
references.context: Preacher's childhood denomination where he first encountered the biblical reference to Leviathan.

references.name: Body of Christ
references.context: Ecclesiological concept invoked to explain the communal dimension of baptism and Christian community as a shared journey.

references.name: Nave (from Latin 'navis'/ship)
references.context: Architectural and etymological reference to the nave of a cathedral as a ship carrying the baptized people of God on a journey together.

references.name: Sacraments across religions
references.context: Reference to how water functions as a sacramental symbol across multiple religious traditions, rooted in natural human experience.

references.name: Biblical symbols and religious imagination
references.context: Discussed how natural elements like water, fire, and air become essential to religious symbolism and sacramental meaning.

primary_theme: Jesus' baptism reveals that Christ enters into the full depths of human existence—both its joys and its dangers—alongside us, reversing the traditional emphasis on human discipleship to show that we are never alone, even in solitude and darkness.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection blending personal memoir (childhood in Guntersville, Alabama) with theological meditation, moving from concrete sensory experience to abstract spiritual principle, using water as an extended metaphor that bridges natural, existential, and theological registers.

references.name: Barbara Brown Taylor
references.context: Cited as 'a wonderful Episcopal priest who's really witty and super smart' who humorously describes what a Christmas pageant based on John's gospel would look like—one child speaking one line before a black velvet curtain.
