references.context: Implicit reference to Christ laying down his life for his friends, described as the Christ-like pattern baptized people commit to.

references.name: The Gospels
references.context: Referenced as a source for understanding Christ's example, though the preacher argues we shouldn't limit ourselves only to Christ's example.

references.name: Trinity
references.context: Invoked to open up theological space beyond limiting ourselves to Christ's male humanity alone, particularly regarding women's ordination.

references.name: Christian theology of incarnation
references.context: The preacher critiques the assumption that Christ's maleness should limit women's ordination, calling this 'an absolute failure of imagination.'

references.name: Anglican Church tradition
references.context: The preacher is an Episcopal priest; references the church's practice of remembering saints and the All Saints tradition.

references.name: All Saints tradition
references.context: A catch-all feast day created by the church to ensure all saints are named and nobody is left out; contrasted with the formal canonization process.

references.name: All Souls tradition
references.context: Related to All Saints; the church's columbarium is named after All Souls, giving permission for personal, unofficial lists of saints.

references.name: Truth and Reconciliation Committee
references.context: Described as one of the greatest social, political, and spiritual experiments, embodying the principle 'forgive and remember' rather than 'forgive and forget.'

references.name: Apartheid in South Africa
references.context: The historical context for Tutu's work; described as a 40-something year systematic oppression of non-white South Africans.

references.name: Theology of God as Creator and Spirit
references.context: God the Creator as source of all being, truth, goodness, and beauty; the Spirit as wisdom and breath moving through all humanity.

references.name: Imago Dei (Image of God)
references.context: The preacher concludes that experiencing wisdom, truth, beauty, and goodness in others allows us to catch a glimpse of the light and image of God.

primary_theme: The preacher encourages listeners to recognize and remember saints—both officially canonized and personally significant—as reflections of God's light, arguing that holiness and divine witness appear not only within the church but throughout humanity and culture.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling combined with pastoral reflection and prophetic critique; the preacher uses a personal anecdote about meeting Desmond Tutu to illustrate theological principles, then invites the congregation to create their own lists of saints from diverse sources (poets, writers, filmmakers, teachers) to challenge conventional boundaries of sacred witness.

references.name: Philosophy of signs vs. symbols
references.context: Philosophers and psychologists make a distinction between signs (singular meaning) and symbols (multivalent, layered meaning); Jesus operates in the symbolic realm while Pharisees are literal-minded.

references.name: The Four Gospels (Matthew, Mark, Luke, John)
references.context: Referenced as sources for portrayal of Pharisees as one-dimensional characters across all four accounts.

references.name: Gospel narrative (Pharisees and taxation question)
references.context: The Pharisees ask Jesus 'Is it lawful to pay taxes?' seeking to trap him; Jesus responds with the coin and the saying about rendering to Caesar and God.

references.name: Matthew 22:15-22 (or parallel: Mark 12:13-17, Luke 20:20-26)
references.context: The specific gospel passage about the denarius coin and Jesus's famous saying: 'Give to the emperor the things that are the emperor's, and to God the things that are God's.'

references.name: Scholarly approach to Jesus and Pharisees
references.context: Modern scholars point out that Jesus and Pharisees share much in common (love of questions, religious dialogue, commentary on philosophy and ritual), complicating the one-dimensional portrayal.

references.name: The concept of 'soul' (ancient and antique term)
references.context: Used to communicate the depth within every person—'so much depth that we ourselves don't even understand completely or fully who we are.'

references.name: Sacramental theology (bread and wine, water)
references.context: Reference to Eucharist and baptism as symbolic acts through which people reveal God's presence and depth.

references.name: Episcopal/Anglican liturgical practice
references.context: References to Eucharist, celebrant role, chapel, and the 11:15 service indicate Anglican/Episcopal ecclesial context.

primary_theme: God's true claim on human life transcends literal, transactional thinking; every person embodies divine presence as an unrepeatable soul and story, calling us to recognize the symbolic depth within ourselves and one another.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative blended with theological reflection—the preacher establishes a philosophical framework (signs vs. symbols), interprets scripture through that lens, then illustrates the theme through personal anecdotes and concrete examples from church life, emphasizing relational encounter over abstract doctrine.

references.name: Isaiah (Prophet)
references.context: Isaiah window features prominently in the cathedral's stained glass; the sermon discusses Isaiah 5 (God's vineyard parable) in relation to current events.

references.name: Isaiah 5 (God's Vineyard)
references.context: God plants a vineyard expecting justice but finds bloodshed; expects righteousness but hears cries of violence—applied to contemporary American violence.

references.name: Isaiah 6 (Coal of Purification)
references.context: An angel places a coal in Isaiah's mouth to purify and inspire him with vision, depicted in the stained glass window below Isaiah's feet.

references.name: Baptismal Covenant
references.context: The preacher invokes the baptismal covenant as calling Christians to advocate for justice and peace among all people.

references.name: Desmond Tutu
references.context: The preacher quotes Tutu: 'When you pray, move your feet'—meaning prayer alone is insufficient without action.

references.name: Bishop Rob O'Neill
references.context: Bishop of Colorado cited as leading a coalition of bishops united against gun violence for over a decade, who released a statement urging lawmakers to ban automatic weapons.

references.name: Charles Connick
references.context: Renowned stained glass window maker from Boston firm who created the cathedral's windows; quoted describing them as 'a playground for the afternoon sun.'

references.name: Christian Sacramental Theology / Eucharistic Theology
references.context: The sermon references coming to the altar table and Eucharistic prayer (Sursum Corda) as invitations to reimagine all of life in light of God's presence.

references.name: Transcendence / Transcendent Light
references.context: The preacher reflects on light as transcendent—not coming from humans but received by humans—as a theological framework for understanding divine presence.

references.name: Hebrew Scripture / Old Testament
references.context: The sermon references prophets from Hebrew scriptures depicted in stained glass, particularly Isaiah as the most prominent figure.

primary_theme: In response to recent violence (Las Vegas shooting), Christians are called to integrate prayer, advocacy for justice, and a reimagined vision of all people in light of God's transcendent presence—a vision made visible through the cathedral's stained glass windows.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with prophetic challenge; uses sensory, visual imagery (light, color, stained glass) as theological anchor; moves from architectural description to scriptural interpretation to contemporary social crisis to spiritual renewal; emphasizes the integration of contemplative seeing with active justice-seeking.

references.name: Genesis 37-50 (Joseph narrative)
references.context: The entire sermon is structured around the Joseph story: his coat of many colors, his dreams, his brothers' jealousy, his sale into Egypt, and his eventual reconciliation with his family.

references.name: Genesis 50:20
references.context: Joseph's deathbed declaration: 'What you intended for evil, God intended for good' - the culminating theological statement of the sermon.

references.name: Jungian analysis / Analytical psychology
references.context: The preacher references an Episcopal priest who was also 'a Jungian analyst' who 'drank from a deep well' and thought deeply about spiritual matters.

references.name: Episcopal Church tradition
references.context: The preacher cites an Episcopal priest as an intellectual and spiritual authority; the sermon is delivered within Episcopal parish context.

references.name: God as experience vs. idea
references.context: The preacher quotes (approvingly, with qualification) a Jungian Episcopal priest's insight that 'God is more of an experience than an idea,' describing this as 'a perfect synopsis of this novella.'

references.name: Hasidic tradition
references.context: The preacher cites a 'great Hasidic saying: God invented people because God loves stories,' using it to frame the theological significance of narrative.

references.name: Literary concept of 'novella'
references.context: The preacher identifies the Joseph narrative as 'the first novella in the Bible' and 'the first long story in Genesis,' applying literary critical analysis to scripture.

references.name: Theological concept of 'reversal'
references.context: The preacher notes Benjamin receiving more food than others as 'another sign of reversal that constantly happens in the scriptures,' indicating awareness of a recurring biblical pattern.

references.name: Concept of divine implication (hiddenness of God)
references.context: The preacher argues that 'God is rarely talked about' in the Joseph story but 'is constantly implied' in tears, feasting, joy, reconciliation, and forgiveness - suggesting a theology of divine hiddenness.

primary_theme: God works redemptively through human relationships and emotional experiences rather than through explicit divine intervention, as demonstrated in the Joseph narrative where God is implied throughout but rarely named.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling with pastoral reflection; the preacher reconstructs the Joseph story in accessible, emotional detail, then moves to theological interpretation and contemporary application through personal pastoral examples (Gwen Scott, Virginia Pine and Perry).

references.name: John's Gospel (Fourth Gospel)
references.context: The sermon's primary biblical text, examining the figure of 'the beloved apostle' and how John's gospel portrays Jesus's love through narrative and strong female characters.

references.name: John 13 (implied—the beloved disciple passage)
references.context: The gospel reading referenced that 'strangely suggests or implies that Jesus has a favorite apostle, the beloved apostle' who is closest to Jesus's heart but anonymous.

references.name: Gospel of Matthew
references.context: Listed as one of the four gospels in comparative discussion; preacher notes John is personal favorite among the four.

references.name: Gospel of Mark
references.context: Listed as one of the four gospels in comparative discussion.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: Listed as one of the four gospels in comparative discussion.

references.name: Samaritan Woman narrative
references.context: Cited as an example of 'strong female characters' in John's Gospel that could support the theory the beloved apostle was female.

references.name: Mary and Martha of Bethany
references.context: Cited as examples of strong female characters in John's Gospel potentially relevant to the beloved apostle's identity.

references.name: Mary Magdalene
references.context: Cited as a strong female character in John's Gospel, used to support the hypothesis that the beloved apostle could be female.

references.name: John the Evangelist (as apostle/disciple)
references.context: Discussed as the likely candidate for the beloved apostle, though questioned on grounds that John the fisherman (brother of James, son of Zebedee) would likely be illiterate.

references.name: James (apostle, brother of John)
references.context: Mentioned as John's brother, both sons of Zebedee the fishermen, used to argue for the unlikelihood that John the evangelist was the author of the fourth gospel.

references.name: Zebedee
references.context: Father of James and John, identified as a fisherman, used contextually to support the argument that John was likely uneducated.

references.name: Contemporary/Modern theologians (unnamed)
references.context: Referenced as suggesting that the beloved apostle could be female, reflecting recent theological scholarship on gender in the gospels.

references.name: Newspaper psychologist (unnamed)
references.context: Author of an article titled 'When a Parent's I Love You Means Do as I Say,' cited for research on conditional parenting and conditional affirmation affecting childhood development.

references.name: Body of research on conditional affirmation
references.context: Cited as 'incredible body of research' demonstrating that children praised only for success grow up less secure with diminished sense of worthiness.

references.name: Episcopal liturgy and hymnody
references.context: Referenced as suggesting that the beloved disciple is John through liturgical tradition and the opening collects for St. John's Day.

references.name: Patriarchal church structure
references.context: Invoked as historical context to explain why John might have made a female beloved apostle anonymous as the early church became more organized and hierarchical.

references.name: Unconditional love
references.context: The central theological concept running throughout the sermon, exemplified in the story of Bob Cook teaching John about 'unconditional love' and Christ's love independent of our actions.

references.name: Episcopalian tradition and priesthood
references.context: Preacher reflects on the Episcopal priesthood, describing his mentor John as 'the archetypal Episcopal priest, the perfect combination of head and heart.'

references.name: Bob Cook (historical figure/mentor)
references.context: Preacher's mentor priest who became the embodiment of unconditional love and shaped the preacher's understanding of priesthood and pastoral care.

primary_theme: Jesus loves us unconditionally, at the level of our being rather than our achievements, as demonstrated through the anonymous beloved apostle in John's Gospel and exemplified through pastoral mentorship.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling with pastoral reflection, moving from biblical scholarship and theological speculation to personal anecdote and psychological research, culminating in a homiletical meditation on unconditional love.

references.name: The Four Gospels
references.context: Preacher references 'one of the funniest and frankly most absurd moments in the four gospels' when the apostles claim to understand Jesus' parables.

references.name: Matthew 13 (Parables of the Kingdom)
references.context: The sermon's primary biblical text: Jesus teaches multiple parables comparing God's kingdom to a mustard seed, leaven, buried treasure, a merchant seeking pearls, and a fishing net sorting good and bad fish.

references.name: Anglican theologian (identified as 'Two Worlds Are Ours')
references.context: Cited for the definition: 'what mysticism really is, it's actually quite ordinary, it's trying to see a deeper reality that is behind, within, and beyond natural phenomena.'

references.name: Mysticism
references.context: Invoked as the theological framework for understanding moments of awe and transcendence within ordinary experience—in cathedrals, mountains, or grocery stores.

references.name: Parable tradition
references.context: The sermon employs the interpretive principle that parables have no single right answer and are meant to provoke personal reflection and imaginative response rather than doctrinal precision.

references.name: Episcopal/Anglican tradition
references.context: Preacher notes that 'Episcopalians are not always good at' loosening up and engaging imaginatively with parables, suggesting a particular ecclesial context for the sermon's hermeneutical approach.

references.name: Continental Divide (geographical/spiritual reference)
references.context: Preacher describes seeing the Continental Divide from Highway near Colorado Springs as 'unbelievable,' invoking the sublime as a threshold moment.

references.name: Pikes Peak (as spiritual/natural phenomenon)
references.context: Standing at the summit with snow visible in summer generates a moment of awe and collective silence, illustrating the mystical principle of encountering the transcendent within creation.

primary_theme: God's infinite mystery is encountered not through intellectual comprehension but through imaginative engagement with ordinary experiences and moments of awe in creation, which prompt personal spiritual reflection.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative with theological reflection; the preacher uses extended storytelling about a youth group hike to Cathedral Ridge as the primary evidence for an abstract theological principle about mysticism, moving from parable interpretation to lived experience.

references.name: Matthew 13:24-30 (Parable of the Weeds)
references.context: The sermon's central biblical text: a farmer plants wheat, an enemy plants weeds at night, and the farmer instructs servants to let both grow together until harvest rather than risk destroying the wheat by pulling weeds prematurely.

references.name: Matthew 13:36-43 (Allegorical Explanation of the Parable)
references.context: The fiery furnace passage that terrifies the preacher; scholars debate whether Jesus himself spoke this explanation or if followers added it later to 'sharpen and allegorize' the original parable.

references.name: Book of Revelation
references.context: Referenced as containing the fiery furnace imagery 'a couple of times' and dismissed as 'a whole other can of worms,' a separate subject from the parable's meaning.

references.name: King James Version (Bible translation)
references.context: Credited with putting the image of Gehenna/hell 'into the English imagination' through its particular translation choices.

references.name: Gehenna / Valley of Hinnom
references.context: The preacher explains that 'Gehenna' is not a metaphysical hell but an actual place—the trash heap outside Jerusalem where Jews burned refuse—making fire a practical rather than eschatological image.

references.name: Four elemental substances (fire, water, earth, wind)
references.context: The preacher notes that fire was one of the four classical elements, helping explain how Jesus' original hearers understood fire as a practical, suggestive metaphor for things needing to be 'let go of' in lives and souls.

references.name: Lectionary / Prayer Book tradition
references.context: The preacher explains that the gospel reading was not his choice but comes from 'the prayer book and that schedule or lectionary that gives us the readings for each Sunday,' establishing the liturgical context.

references.name: Episcopal theology / practice
references.context: The preacher invokes Episcopal identity when noting that 'we should be good at this as Episcopalians' at putting terrifying images 'in some type of context intellectually.'

references.name: Scholarly biblical criticism (source criticism)
references.context: The preacher reports that 'some scholars even believe that the fiery furnace bit and this long explanation...was actually not spoken by Jesus himself, but was instead added later by followers of Jesus,' demonstrating awareness of form-critical scholarship.

references.name: Wisdom literature tradition
references.context: Jack's retrospective reflection on decades of life choices exemplifies the wisdom tradition of learning through experience and age; the preacher suggests 'we don't have to wait that long if you don't want to' for such wisdom.

references.name: Addiction as 'loving the good more than we should'
references.context: The preacher suggests that addiction might be defined as 'loving the good more than we should,' reflecting on how 'wheat can turn into weeds' when excellence becomes excess.

primary_theme: Patience, humility, and the acceptance of moral ambiguity in life: the parable teaches that we cannot always clearly separate good from evil in ourselves or others, and premature judgment risks greater loss than waiting for proper discernment.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection combined with scholarly biblical exposition; the preacher uses personal storytelling (the letter from Jack, his search process for the parish) to illustrate abstract theological principles, alternating between intellectual analysis (etymology of Gehenna, scholarly theories about authorship) and emotional resonance (the terror of hellfire imagery, the wisdom of lived experience).
